Open Webinar 	STRUCTURE
Personal 	Endorsement 	of Host for Guest/Seller
"Tease" 	Reasons to 	Stay 	Tuned
Establish 	Authority 	of Seller
Establish 	Specific Relevance 	of Seller to 	Audience
Establish Over-Arching 	Promise
Tease 	Reason(s) To 	Stay 	Tuned
Begin 	Explanation 	of Product/Proposition
-Promise 	within the promise
:e»>-~f
)$~~ 	~ 	.../(
-"Reveal" 	Some 	Surprising/Contrarian/Appealing "Fact"
Continue 	Explanation- 	and Gain 	Agreement to 	"The 	#1 Premise"
7F
Begin 	Separating 	Audience 	Into 	"Winners 	and 	Losers" - 	to be verified 	by 	their
reaction 	to 	the proposition .... add 	"Who 	Is 	This For" 	.... and 	within 	that, 	Overcome 	Top
Objection(s)
Present 	Offer 	- 	what they get
Make 	product/Offer Specifically Relevant 	To 	Audience
Present 	Price
Add 	Bonuses
(FIRSn 	CALL 	TO 	ACTION
Social 	Proof
(SECOND) 	CALL 	TO 	ACTION
(THIRD) 	CALL 	TO 	ACTION
End with Personal Endorsement 	Of Host

DAN: 	Thank 	you 	Michael. This is my 	35t h Year in the success business, as an author,
consultant, coach and speaker - but throughout that time 	I've 	also been a hands-on serial
entrepreneur, and 	I've 	built, bought and sold businesses. I 	had 	an early bruising, nose
bloodying bankruptcy. Recovered nicely. In my area 	of expertise, 	I'm 	the highest paid
consultant, getting from 	$100,000.00 	to $1-million per project; 	$18,800.00 	for an initial
day's 	conversation. The publishing company and membership organization built around
me 	has over 	25,000 	members worldwide, 	300,000 	online subscribers and customers, even
100 	local study groups. 	I'm 	an investor in and strategic advisor 	to 	a handful 	of emerging
companies, including a software company on the Inc. 	500 	list, InfusionSoft, and
Kennedys Barber Clubs, a franchised chain 	of upscale 	men's 	barber 	shops- 	with 5 open,
2 opening currently, over 	50 	under contract to open ... .in the existent shops, over 	1,500
members. They pay by the month, not by the haircut. Anyway, over these years, 	I've
worked intimately with hundreds, and in some way, with thousands 	of what I call
'Renegade Millionaires': first generation, from scratch millionaire and multi-millionaire
entrepreneurs and 7-figure yearly income earners who all share a particular mindset, but
more importantly practice a small collection 	of little-understood strategies ..... not vague
ideas, 	but 	strategies ..... that produce their success in very diverse fields. By synthesizing
and applying these, 	I've 	made myself financially independent at a still young

DAN: 	The origin, goes back to Napoleon Hill, and really 	beyond- 	but most people will
be most familiar with Napoleon Hill and THINK AND 	GROW 	RJCH. 	At America's first
bona fide, from scratch Billionaire, Andrew Carnegie's request, Hill worked from 1917
to 1936 on research .... meeting with, interviewing, analyzing .... hundreds 	of the era's
greatest achievers to IDENTIFY THE 	FEW, 	CRITICAL 	COMMONALITIES 	in their
thought processes and philosophies. The result was first published 	as 	THE 	LAWS 	OF
SUCCESS, 	about a philosophy; then as the popular book THINK AND 	GROW 	RICH.
And 	it 	has endured as one 	of the most influential books and is fine 	as 	far 	as 	it goes,
although an early rrientor 	of mine 	sai4: 	you can't 	eat 	philosophy. I helped with the
original Think And Grow Rich television infomercial, featuring Hall 	ofF arne 	quarterback
Fran Tarkenton, incidentally. 	I was inspired 	by 	the modus operandi; the method; the
basic idea 	of identifying commonalities 	in enough people who had what I wanted -
including wealth, lived 	as 	I wanted 	to 	live .. .those commonalities revealing principles.
But I also made a major discovery that I call "beyond Think And Grow Rich". 	It 	is one 	of
the seven chief strategies in my Renegade Millionaire System. Would 	you 	like me to
1. h 	? 	I
revea 	It 	ere. 	/,., 	L- 	1, 	z• 	,

MICHAEL: 	If you're 	willing 	to 	go 	that 	far, 	on 	this 	call- 	absoliltel 	}'I' I$ 	) 	1\A- 	f' ,..~
DAN: 	Okay. 	People should grab a pen and paper or this. It's 	big. 	It's called
BEHAVIORAL 	CONGRUENCY. 	Let me back up, and take 	us 	to 	it. 	My 	big discovery
was that major 	success- 	in money, in business, in 	life- 	was not 	so 	much 	about what or
how you think, 	or 	about the particular opportunity you chose or found, 	or 	any 	of the
commonly believed in differentiators like childhood, education, location or timing -
instead, major success in life 	is .NOT 	about what you think, but 	about what you 	DO.
About 	behavior. 	The writer-down'er 	is: 	it's about what you 	DO, 	so 	you have total control
through the power 	of present day, personal choice. But the big breakthrough to be had
from this .... highly successful people who have whatever it is you'd love to have, who
live as you'd love.to live ... .if you could study enough 	of them ....you'd 	find 	they share
certain key 	BEHAVIORS 	in common, and 	if you bring 	your 	behaviors in sync with
theirs, then you 	automqtical!J! 	get and quite easily attract the very same results they get.
And the change is very guick. What I call 'The Phenomenon'. 	So, 	for example, I don't
fly commercial; I fly by private jet- with no hassles, on 	my 	schedule. I don't work any
fixed hours and I have a 5-minute commute 	to 	my home office, and can make money
sitting out on 	my 	backyard deck watching deer and squirrels in the summer, 	in 	my
recliner 	by 	the frreplace in the winter, talking on the phone. 	I'm 	independent; 	I never 	_
need another earned dollar. 	I'm 	a sought after expert; people want to do business 	with 	me
and only 	with 	me. 	I'm 	not alone. There are quite a few people who share these life

The 	even 	bigger discovery is that this scientific approach to behavioral congruency is
something that high achievers 	consciously 	and 	deliberately
use, and 	you 	can too. 	It 	can be laid out logically, step by step, as 	I've 	done in
The 	Renegade Millionaire System, and followed to be your 	own 	success architect,
drawing up blueprints, to get whatever 	you 	want. 	And make no mistake: 	my 	System 	is
about 	WHAT 	TO 	DO, practical 	strategies to 	make 	what 	now 	seems hard easy, and
SPEED 	UP 	your success. My friend, the late Jim Newman, author 	of the book 'Release
Your 	Brakes', said that most people are trying to go down the 	road 	with one foot 	on 	the
gas but the other foot riding the brake - that 	you 	need to release the brake to zoom ahead.
The 	foot 	on 	the brake 	is 	in clinging to old, inappropriate behaviors that are 	not 	congruent
with 	the 	success you want, the goals 	you 	have, the income 	you 	want, the kind 	of business
you 	want. Stepping 	on 	the gas, well, that's using Behavioral Conrgruency.

-- 7 of 122 --

You want to get the power 	of attraction on your side. How do you do 	THAT? 	Well, not
by 	meditating 	about attraction. You 	do 	it 	by 	marketing yourself, not your 	thing- 	so
that .... here's the writer-downer .... so 	that people are eager to 	do 	business 	with 	YOU. 	So
that they are receptive to 	YOU. 	So that they seek 	YOU 	out.
Trump is a top tier illustration. 	I've 	spoken on programs with The Donald, with Ivanka,
and with Trump's chief negotiator, George Ross. I've made something 	of a study 	of him.
I 	think 	he's 	fascinating. There's even a Chapter in my autobiography, 'Unfinished
Business' titled: 	Why 	I 	Won't Do Business With Donald 	Trump- 	and, by the way, some
people watching this webinar will get that book, my autobiography 	as 	a gift. Here's
something you've hopefully noticed: one 	of the 	THINGS 	he gets money for is attaching
his name 	to, co-developing, 	and 	partnering in real estate. But that's not the real business
he's 	in, nor is 	it 	his major focus. Instead, Trump is in the Trump business. Marketing
himself.
Of course 	you're quick to say: 	I'm 	not 	Donald 	Tn.unp. 	Well, poor you. Hey, he wasn't
Donald Trump either until he got busy 	-with 	certain behaviors- and made himself into
Donald Trump. Second 	of all, you probably don't need to be 	as 	splashy or famous as
Trump or to be Trump to have everything 	in 	life you want. I don't. His appetite's bigger
than mine. But certain behaviors 	of his belong as yours, deliberately MODELED.
Behavioral Modeling, by the way, is something I 	lay 	out for you in my Renegade
Millionaire System. And one 	of the FREE Reports that I've prepared specifically for you
is 	about making yourself into, and marketing yourself as THE person others want to 	do
business 	with- 	as a real estate investor and entrepreneur.
Self-marketers engage in certain very specific strategies and behaviors. I put them all in
my Renegade Millionaire System. And 	if Darin 	Garman 	does get to us and dial in, 	he 	can
speak to this from personal experience. It changed his life.
MICHAEL: 	You've 	talked 	of seven 	categories 	of stra 	from 	your 	Renegade
Millionaire 	System. 	I can't 	i 	anybody 	not 	eager 	to 	get 	into 	nderstand 	all 	seven, 	and 	in

First, 	though, 	you 	told 	me 	before 	this 	webrnar, 	rather 	bluntly, 	that 	your 	System 	would 	NOT 	appeal

way .... and 	that 	your 	System 	was 	"for" 	a very 	particular 	kind 	of 	person. 	Why 	don't 	you 	tell 	people
about 	that?
DAN: 	There are two failure-behaviors a lot 	of people 	are 	absolutely ADDICTED 	to, 	that
border on mental psychosis, that sabotage their ambitions again and again. These addicts
will not find the case 	I'm 	making here, about how to release the brakes and speed up
success, persuasive. Many such addicts have left 	us 	already. But let me quickly describe
these harmful addictions:
Failure Behavior #I: Bright Shiny 	Object 	Syndrome. The person with this syndrome 	is
often a lifelong, compulsive seminar junkie and next-big-thing buyer, with shelves 	full 	of
notebooks and CD's ... but 	he 	never really applies himself to 	anything, 	because he's 	so

List~n, 	I'm 	aware. 	I?ost 	of the people on this webinar have a tendency to invest in very

sec~et 	source 	of pre, pre-freclosures. 	How 	to 	flip any property in 72 hours. I get this
destre, 	and 	I acknowledge, that is 	NOT 	what 	I'm 	offering here. But. ..
Bluntl~, 	if d~i11g 	th~t 	and 	that 	alone 	hasn't 	gotten you 	up 	to the 	mountai~-top, 	even left
you 	a 	little 	disappointed 	and frustrated, 	I'm 	here to tell you, 	you 	are missin cr 	a critical
element, missing 	out 	on 	the bigger picture... 0
The 	word 	SYSTEM 	in 	Renegade Millionaire System 	is 	key. Most people 	know 	they need
a 	system 	for doing their things. 	Pilots 	have checklists. 	You 	have a system for appraising a
property's 	value. 	For 	presenting your proposition to 	an 	owner. McDonalds has a system
for 	making 	burgers, frying fries, selling franchises. 	But 	you most 	NEED 	a 	SYSTEM 	for
managing 	yourself successfully, 	to 	make your 	own 	business and personal practices and
behaviors congruent 	and 	in 	sync with the behaviors 	of the people already achieving the
results 	you 	want. 	In 	my 	SYSTEM, 	I teach 	you 	how 	to do specific Modeling, and I give
you 	the 	Model 	of the Renegade Millionaire: the person making all the money he wants
while living life 	on 	his 	own 	tem1s.
Do 	you 	know 	what 	this number is?
77.5. 	It's 	the 	average life expectancy 	of the American male. My odomoter just 	turned 55,
so I 	have 	22.5 years left, less any lurking, unpleasant surprises; I am a non-insulin-
dependent diabetic; 	and 	plus any bonuses. You should do your own math. And then
ponder, 	how 	many 	of those remaining years, months, days, hours are you willing to give
up 	to 	work 	you 	don't 	care about or find unfulfilling ..... compromises you wish you 	didn't
have 	to 	make ..... people, as boss, customers, whoever, 	you 	would rather not deal with?
Because a big point 	of my 	System 	is 	AUTONOMY, 	now, not someday. Autonomy
means: doing business 	with 	who 	you 	want, when you want; under your terms; working
when 	and 	where you 	want- 	without being controlled 	by 	others or compelled by financial
need. 	This 	is a position 	you 	get 	into 	by 	means 	of your very own behavioral choices, best
made 	by 	modeling others achieving what 	you 	want; best adhered to 	by 	system, not
random 	chance.
I 	own 	and, 	in 	the summer drive two classic cars - a 1965 Lincoln-Continental convertible
and a vintage 	AMC 	Javelin 	SST. 	As 	my 	main car, I drive a 	2006 	Ford Explorer, with
less 	than 	25 	000 	miles 	on 	it .... actually a gift from a client 	-not 	a Lexus or Mercedes. I
have 	no 	need 	to buy a 	new 	car to impress anybody. I have the luxury 	of buying the
classic cars I want. 	This 	is autonomy. 	Few 	people set 	out 	by deliberate design to create
and 	achieve autonomy. 	If you 	will 	let 	me, I will show 	you 	how 	to do this very rare thing.
This 	System, then, is for 	the person who sees 	them~elves 	as 	deservi~g 	of su~h 	autonomy
in 	every 	aspect 	of their business life 	an? 	perso~al 	hfe, and .values 	being 	advised and 	_
mentored about this 	by 	somebody who s done It and does 	It.

applied them in 	136 different business categories.
So 	you'll 	be hard-pressed to 	be 	in 	one 	or 	even• 	•
1mag1ne 	one where these strategies don't 	work
and 	don't work very 	well.
•
Here's what we're going to 	do. 	If 	you 	own 	a
. business, then 	on 	your 	list 	of things that 	you 	hate,
right 	up 	towards 	the 	top should 	be 	what 	I 	call 	being
"an 	advertising 	victim.'' 	That's 	when 	you 	get the 	big,
black checkbook out of the center desk 	drawer, 	and
you 	sit down across the desk 	from 	some 	media
salesperson, 	and 	you 	write out 	some 	kind 	of a check
for some amount for some 	kind 	of promotion
advertising expense, 	and 	you 	have 	no 	earthly idea
whether 	,YOU 	made a good decision, 	bad 	decision,
when 	you'll 	know, 	how 	you'll 	know, 	if 	you'll 	know. 	I
detest that kind of uncertainty, don't like 	to 	play
guesswork games with 	my 	money. 	Bet 	you 	don't
either. 	I'm 	going to show 	you 	how 	to eliminate 	it,
how to make every 	dollar 	you 	spend 	on 	advertising
and 	marketing measurable 	and 	accountable.
lf 	you 	sell 	for a 	living, 	then right up 	on 	top 	of 	your
hate 	list 	should 	be 	what 	I 	call 	"cold-call 	grunt 	work."
Now, 	my 	friend 	Zig 	calls 	that 	"warm 	approaching."
And 	if 	he 	and 	I 	agreed 	on 	everything, 	one of us
would be unnecessary. 	I 	see.·	.. 	I 	see 	absolutely
nothing warm, friendly, 	fuzzy, 	happy about the
process of grinding it 	out, 	talking to 	people 	who 	-
at least emotionally, 	if 	not 	physically- 	are 	backing
away 	from 	you as fast 	as 	you're 	moving 	towards
them. 	To 	me, 	cold prospecting 	is like coal 	mining.
Dirty, ugly, 	filthy, 	smelly 	work 	best 	left to 	people
who 	make minimum 	wage 	with 	brawn, 	not 	maximum
wage 	with 	brain.
So 	regardless of what you've done 	in 	the past 	and
regardless 	of 	what your sales manager has 	told 	you,
even 	if 	that sales manager is or 	was 	sitting next 	to
you 	today. 	As a 	result 	of what 	we 	do 	here this
afternoon, 	you 	should 	never make a cold 	call 	again
as 	long 	as you 	live. 	I'm 	going to 	show 	you 	how 	to
eliminate 	that from your sales activity.
1have one quick story for you, set the stage for
where we're going to 	go. 	and 	then 	we'll 	roll 	up 	our
sleeves and get 	to 	work. 	I 	live in Phoenix. 	And 	in
Phoenix, 	we have a thing called the 	sun. 	You 	see 	it
sort of filtered 	here, 	but 	it 	is a bright 	orang~. ball 	when
you 	see 	it 	under other conditions. 	And 	our 	conditions
are very hot. 	It's exactly what you've heard, 	if 	you've
never been there. 	It's 	a 	dry 	heat, 	like a 	microwave.
When 	1called 	the office 	today, 	we're at 	107. 	It's

•
important 	to 	this 	story. 	I travel a 	lot, 	200,000 	air
miles 	in 	a 	typical 	year. 	But 	1do 	have 	an 	office, 	do
have 	a 	staff. 	Even 	when 	I'm 	home 	in 	Phoenix 	though,
I tend not to 	go 	to 	the office. 	I find it disturbs the 	staff.
•
So 	generally, 	when 	I'm 	home, 	I 	stay 	home. 	And 	one
of 	the 	things 	I 	do 	is 	catch 	up 	on 	telephone 	calls 	with
my 	personal clients. 	And 	so, 	4 	July's 	ago, 	I'm 	home
alone 	on 	a 	weekday afternoon. 	Everybody's 	out 	of
the 	house. 	I'm 	at 	the 	kitchen 	counter. 	I've 	got a
pitcher of 	iced 	tea, 	f 've got the 	client 	on 	a speaker
phone, 	I'm 	intensely 	involved 	in 	a conversation 	with
the 	client, 	when 	someone 	unknown to 	me, 	uninvited,
unannounced 	and 	unexpected begins to 	ring 	the
door 	bell 	and 	bang 	on 	the front doors 	of 	my 	home
with 	earthquake intensity.
Now, 	you 	all 	work 	for a 	real 	living. 	You're not home
during 	the 	day, 	on 	a 	weekday, 	so 	you'll 	have 	to 	stop
and 	think 	about 	this. 	But 	the options of who 	can 	be
at 	your home 	door, 	unannounced, unexpected 	and
uninvited 	in 	the middle 	of 	a weekday 	afternoon are
very 	small. 	It's 	a 	pest. 	I 	did 	what you 	do 	with 	pests.
I 	ignored 	it, 	confident that 	if 	sufficiently ignored, 	it 	·
would 	go 	pester somebody else.
So 	I'm carrying 	on 	my telephone conversation, 	and
this 	person 	is 	ringing 	and 	banging 	and ringing and
banging 	and 	ringing 	and 	banging 	with 	surprising
persistence. 	But 	finally, 	I 	win. 	He 	goes around ...
He 	leaves, 	ever 	so 	briefly. 	He 	goes around to the
rear of 	my 	property, 	he 	climbs 	over 	an 	8-foot 	high
masonry 	wall 	with 	shards of glass embedded 	in 	the
top of 	it 	to 	discourage this method of 	entry. 	He
comes down past the cactuses, the shrubs, the
pool 	and 	the 	spa, 	and 	he 	is now on the patio deck
immediately 	behind 	me. 	He 	can 	see 	my 	back to
him 	through the panes of glass in the patio doors
on which he is now 	banging 	with 	incredible intensity.
This is 	by 	far the most annoying pest 	ever. 	But 	I'm
like the 	most 	stubborn guy 	ever. 	So 	I 	keep 	my 	back
to this person 	and 	I continue 	my 	conversation,
having to raise 	my 	voice 	and 	almost 	yell 	into the
speakerphone to 	be 	heard. He continues to 	bang
and 	bang 	and 	bang. 	And 	finally, 	I 	can't stand 	it
anymore. 	I 	tum 	around 	to 	deal 	with 	the most
annoying pest 	ever, 	and 	the reason he's there is
my 	entire backyard is 	in 	flames. 	We 	set a record
that 	year- 	15 	str~ight 	115° 	days, everything a
little 	dry 	and brittle. 	My 	guess is some fool driving
through the neighborhood 	flicked 	a cigarette butt.
I'll 	never 	know.

~ut 	literally 	everything but the 	water 	in 	the 	pool 	is 	on

In 	this 	case, 	..Call 	911 	stupid, 	while 	I 	work 	the 	hose."
Now, 	the 	reason 	it's instructive is 	because 	know 	it 	or
not, 	acknowledge 	it 	or 	not, 	like 	hearing 	it 	or 	not, 	most
of 	the 	time 	you 	do the 	opposite. 	You 	are 	perceived
by 	your prospects, 	customers, 	clients, 	past, 	present
and 	future, 	as a 	pest, 	not 	as 	the 	most 	welcomed
guest of their 	day, 	week, 	month 	or 	year. 	And 	if 	you
cc;>ufd 	change 	that, 	if 	you 	could 	get 	the 	kind 	of
welcome 	from your prospective customers or clients
that 	you 	gave 	President 	Ford 	here 	this afternoon, that
would 	change your entire experience of 	doing
business. 	I 	call 	it 	"fixing 	the first 	square 	on 	the
marketing game 	board."

•
I 	have 	two 	friends 	in 	this 	business, 	the speaking

number 	of tapes in the 	album.

So 	that's what 	I 	do for organizations 	and 	individuals.
As 	a consultant, 	I build 	those 	kinds 	of 	marketing
systems. 	They 	have three 	parts 	and 	only three 	parts.
We're going to 	talk 	about 	all 	three 	today 	very 	quickly,
in 	the 	limited 	time 	we 	have 	available. 	Anytime 	we
build 	a marketing 	system, 	here's 	what 	it 	looks 	like.
Here's the three 	parts:
The first one has to 	do 	with 	the 	marketing 	message.
What 	is 	it that 	you 	say 	to 	the 	marketplace that is
compelling, 	that 	is 	curiosity- 	and 	interest-arousing,
that 	is 	magnetic, that causes 	your 	perfect prospects
·	, 	to flock to 	you? 	Do 	you 	have that 	kind 	of a 	message?
We're going to ask 	some 	questions 	and 	find 	out.
,
Secondly, 	who 	you 	say 	it 	to, 	and 	by 	deliberate 	strategy,
who 	you 	don't. The issue of target 	marketing, 	where
you 	get 	all 	your 	leverage.
Third, 	how 	you 	deliver the marketing 	message to the
chosen 	prospects, that's 	called 	media. 	Do 	you 	use
effective 	m~ia? 	Do 	you 	use 	efficient 	media? 	Do
you 	really 	have 	a 	system 	that 	is consistent 	and
reliable? 	We're 	going 	to 	take a 	look 	at that 	and
find 	out.
When 	you 	put those 3 	pieces 	together, 	you 	get a
marketing 	system 	that often transforms the results of
businesses, 	multiples 	of 	2, 	3, 	4 times 	what 	they've 	been
doing. 	No 	one 	of 	those steps 	is 	any 	more 	important
than 	the 	other. 	It's 	like 	a 	3-legged 	stool. 	But 	we'll
take 	them 	in 	order.
The.first 	one, 	message. 	Under 	message, 	I'm 	going
to 	give 	you 	something to 	think 	about. 	I'm 	going 	to
give 	you 	a 	homework 	assignment. This is the 	single
most important question anybody 	will 	ever 	ask 	you
about your 	product, 	service or 	business, 	as 	long 	as
you're associated 	with 	it. 	This single 	most 	important
question, 	as 	you 	will 	see, 	can take 	tiny 	business,

make them 	big 	businesses, can make marketing easy
when 	it 	was once 	hard. 	This single question, 	I'll 	give
you 	an 	example,·a demonstration of just how powerful
it 	can 	be. 	For those of you taking notes, the technical
term for the answer is 	USP. 	Jt 	stands for 	Unique
Selling 	Proposition, one of the greatest marketing
weapons you 	can 	ever create tor your product or
business.
\·
'
And 	here's the question: 	Why 	should 	r choose 	to 	do
business with 	you 	versus any 	and 	every other option
available 	to me 	in 	your category? 	Why 	should 	I
choose to do business 	with 	you 	versus any 	and
every other option 	available 	to 	me 	in 	your category?
When 	you 	have a great answer 	to 	that question, you
can 	kill 	the competition 	and 	you 	can 	dominate your
market, as this story 	will 	demonstrate.
There's a 	college 	kid... 	Many of you 	will 	know 	this
story. 	College 	kid, 	trying to get through 	college, 	no
family, 	no 	family resources, no 	money, 	no 	scholarship,
.
•
winds 	up 	taking 	control 	of a 	failing 	little 	business on 	:-"\
the 	edge 	of 	college 	campus, 	that the owner is 	only

idea is how he's going 	to 	get 	throug~ 	school 	is 	~
working this business. 	He 	and 	a 	buddy. 	Halfway 	-~
through the 	project, 	ifs 	still 	losing 	money, 	not 	\l
making 	money. 	His 	partner 	bails 	out 	and 	leaves
him 	alone.. 	He 	winds 	up 	having to drop out of school 	~
to try to make this 	work 	and 	pay 	off the 	accumulated 	~
debts 	on 	the strength of a Unique 	Selling 	Proposition
he 	then 	invents. 	A 	single sentence. 	~
He not only turns that crummy 	little 	business 	around
from 	losses to 	profits. 	Quickly, 	in 	his category, 	he
dominates his city, his 	state, 	his country. 	And
according to 	Fortune 	Magazine, 	in 	under 10 	years,
he becomes one of the 	1,000 	wealthiest citizens 	on
the planet.
.
His 	Unique 	Selling 	Proposition was 	so 	powertul 	that
for 10 	years, you 	could 	go 	out anywhere 	in 	North
Am~rica, 	stop 100 	people 	at 	random 	on 	the 	street,
play 	word 	association 	with 	them 1 	give 	them 	the 	,
generic 	equivalent 	of his product, 	and 	80 	or 	more 	of
the 	100, 	the first thing on the 	tip 	of 	their tongue was
his proprietary brand narrie and 	what 	a good job he did .
•

ordinary, mundane business. 	he 	set the industry•

delivered 	in 	30 	minutes 	or 	less, 	guaranteed.,. 	And 	on

Manahan had revolutionized his industry.

first 	thing 	to notice is that 	Tom 	didn•t 	try 	to 	promise 	all

t~at 	all 	his 	competitors 	did 	badly, 	that 	annoyed the

'Til 	get your pizza 	quick. 	soon, 	fast, fast,	er 	than 	the
other 	~guy, 	faster 	than 	a speeding 	bullet." 	Tom 	said,
"Set 	your 	watch. 	It 	will 	be 	there 	in 	precisely 	30 	minutes

With 	2.0/20 	hindsight, 	Tom 	and 	I 	both 	agree he 	would
have 	better off saying 	33 	minutes, 	37 	minutes. 	The
drivers 	could 	have 	used 	the extra 	minutes. 	And 	the
odd 	number 	sounds 	even 	more 	specific 	than the 	even.
But 	conceptually, 	it's 	called 	"gutsy, 	accountable
marketing," 	a 	way 	for 	the 	consumer to 	hold 	the
merchant's feet to the 	fire. 	Very 	few 	marketers.are
willing 	to do 	it 	for 	obvious 	reasons. 	Those 	who 	do
gain 	enormous 	leverage.
Third, a guarantee. 	All 	3 of those things 	knit together
in 	this tight, 	concise statement 	we 	call 	a 	.Unique 	Selling
Proposition 	gave 	Tom 	Monahan 	the 	power, 	the
leverage, 	to 	dominate 	an 	industry. 	Now, 	you 	may
not want to dominate 	an 	industry. 	You 	may 	not 	want
to 	dominate 	the 	globe. 	But 	this 	is a 	pretty 	good 	way 	to
dominate business 	in 	your 	own 	town, as 	well. 	It 	starts
with 	a 	Unique 	Selling 	Proposition. That's 	the
foundation, 	the 	solid 	foundation 	we 	build 	a 	marketing
message 	on. 	If 	you 	don't 	have 	it, 	you're 	building 	on
shifting 	sand.
Well, 	for the sake of 	our 	limited 	time. 	let's 	assume 	we
figure out this first thing. 	Let's 	assume 	we 	have 	a
powerlul 	marketing 	message, 	we're 	read 	to 	et 	it 	out
to 	the marketplace. 	Her 	' 	next 	challen
The world's greatest 	market1hg 	-...~5sage 	is 	no 	better
than 	the world's 	worst marketing 	message. 	If 	it 	is
shouted 	at, 	deaf ears. 	We 	do 	this a 	tot. 	I 	call 	it 	wasting
bullets on 	targets. 	And 	even 	if 	you 	hit 	them, 	it doesn't

Most 	people's 	marketing 	is 	not very 	sophisticated at 	all.
Here's 	what 	it 	looks 	like. 	Print up 	a 	brochure. 	A 	lot 	of
them. 	Put them 	in 	a 	burlap 	sack. 	Rent a 	plane. 	Fly 	low.

the issue of target marketing. 	Target 	is where
all 	your leverage comes 	from. 	It' 	you 	turn 	small

Now, 	if 	you 	know 	nothing 	else 	about target 	marketing,
here's what 	I 	hope 	will 	happen. 	In 	the 	few 	minutes
we're going 	to 	talk 	about 	it, Jhope 	you'll 	become

your 	particular 	business, 	which 	in 	most 	cases 	is a
do-it-yourself 	project. 	mostly 	for 	free, 	if 	not 	entirely
for 	free - 	take 	you 	about 	a day - that 	will 	give 	you 	.
the 	leverage to 	become 	a 	sophisticated target 	marketer:
If 	you 	know nothing 	else 	about 	it, 	first of 	all, 	business-
to-business marketers 	do 	it 	a 	little 	differently 	technical
than consumer marketers. 	Conceptually, 	it's 	all 	the
same. 	And 	if 	you 	know 	nothing 	else 	about 	it, 	yo_, ..
understand the first thing 	on 	the 	list.
• .
These are 	th 	5 prima 	wa 	s of 	15 	way~s· hat
people 	can 	do 	rge 	·marketing. 	And 	if 	you 	k 1711 ,.,
nothing 	else 	about 	it, 	the 	first 	one 	eve 	•ro,.
understands. 	lfs 	basic geographic 	arketing.
You 	get a 	map, 	you 	take a 	Styrofoam 	ee 	cup,
tum 	the 	cup upside 	down 	over your 	your
store, your factory, your 	place 	of 	ness. 	You
take the kid's crayon, draw a 	around 	the 	cup,
take the cup 	away, 	that's 	my 	et 	market. 	And 	it's
valid, but it's dangerous 	asteful 	in 	its simplicity,
which is why 	I want 	you 	become 	much 	more
sophisticated. 	me 	explain 	to 	you 	.
One 	day 	I'm 	in 	my office, 	and 	I get a phone call.
Now 	you 	should 	know, 	I'm 	never 	in 	my 	office.
And 	when 	I'm 	there, 	I never take incoming 	calls
for reasons that 	will 	become 	obvious 	in 	a 	second.
But this 	day, 	somehow this 	guy 	gets 	through 	to 	me,
gets me 	on 	the 	phone. 	He 	says, 	"Look, 	I've 	been 	to
three of your seminars. 	I've 	got your 	complete
marketing system. 	Most 	recently, 	we've 	been 	doing a
mailing 	campaign exactly as you 	teach 	us to 	do 	it 	for
our business, 	and 	we have 	been 	getting 	zero
response." This is not a good 	call. 	This 	is 	why 	I
don't take incoming 	calls. 	But this 	day, 	somehow
this guy gets through 	to 	me .
•
So 	I've 	got to start asking questions: Turns out he
owns the largest carpet 	cleaning 	company 	in 	Phoenix,
where 	I 	live. 	I 	said, 	"Okay, 	where have 	you 	been
sending 	all 	the 	mail 	that's not doing 	so 	well?" 	He
said, 	"We 	picked some neighborhoods 	real 	close 	to
the 	plant, 	so 	it 	would 	be 	easy for the guys 	in 	the trucks
to take care of the customers." 	Geographic. target
marketing. 	I 	said, 	"Okay, 	what 	can 	you 	tell 	me 	about
the people 	who 	live 	there?" 	He 	said, 	uThey 	live close
to 	the 	plant." 	I 	had 	that. 	I was moving 	on 	to 	gee-
demographics and geopsychographics. 	Big 	words,
simple ideas.

-- 23 of 122 --

Demographics are statistics about people, because
birds 	of 	a feather flock together. 	People 	who 	are alike
live together. 	Demographics are 	like 	income of the
household, 	occupation of the household, 	do 	they have
kids, 	do 	they 	not 	have 	kids. 	Psychographical are
behaviorar: what 	people 	want, what 	people 	desire,
what people 	are 	interested 	in, 	and 	what people
spend their money 	on , what magazines do they
subscribe 	to, 	where do they 	go, 	what TV programs
do 	they 	w 	h. 	All 	this information is rea i
available 	f 	you 	know 	w 	ere to 	look 	for it 	and
ow 	to 	et 	i . 	In 	fact, the 	most 	important thing
to 	understand about target marketing is that
privacy 	in 	America is dead. 	Bad 	news 	tor
those of us who 	like 	privacy, good news
for marketers.
If .you 	say to 	me, 	"My 	ideal prospect is a one-legged
midget between the ages of 36 	and 	52 	with 	an
industrial engineering 	degree, 	4 kids, 3 	dogs, 	2
television sets, drives 	an 	AMC 	Pacer 	and 	bowls 	on
Wednesday night," 	I can 	get 	you 	a list of those 	;n
your 	zip 	code. 	It's 	not 	going to 	be 	a very 	big 	list. ..
yeah, okay. 	But 	J can 	get 	you 	the 	fist. 	Privacy 	in
America is dead.
You 	can clone your customers 	if 	you 	know 	who 	and
what 	you 	want 	to 	clone. 	Therets 	a 	key 	word. This is
a 	bad 	word in every other 	application 	but 	here. 	Here
it's 	a 	million-dollar 	mart<eting 	word. 	The 	word 	is
"discriminate." 	What 	you 	want to do as a marketer is
figure out how to discriminate 	as 	early 	in 	the process,
as cheaply as 	possible, 	so 	that 	you 	devote all 	of your
resources only 	to 	the 	high-probability 	prospects.
•
Let's assume 	you 	and 	I own the carpet cleaning
company 	and 	we're going to 	mail 	into 	these
neighborhoods, which 	in 	and of itself is 	open 	to
question. 	But 	if 	we 	are, 	what do we want to 	know,
how 	do 	we 	want 	to 	discriminate? 	We 	probably
want to mail 	only 	to homeowners, not renters. 	We
probably want to 	mail 	only 	to homes valued at X 	and
above, 	and omit 	all 	of those 	valued 	at 	Y and 	befow.
We 	might 	want 	to 	mail only to head-of-household
occupations more 	likely 	than others to entertain at
home. They 	need 	more 	~arpet 	cleaning. 	We 	might
want to 	mail 	only to 	families 	that have kids 	and 	pets.
They make more 	messes. 	They 	need 	more carpet
cleaning. 	We 	might want to 	mail 	to people 	who
subscribe to magazines 	like 	Manha 	Stewart Living,
Good Housekeeping, 	House 	Beautiful, 	inaicating 	an
interest in a 	clean 	house. 	We 	might want to 	mail 	to
people 	who have credit 	cards, 	so 	they 	can 	pay 	us.

make a living, 	but 	I'm 	a 	high 	school graduate.
Now, 	this particular 	system 	I'm 	going 	to 	show 	you)
I 	stole the 	basis. . . 	t 	borrowed the 	basis 	for 	it 	by
observation 	only, 	some ... 	almost 	30 	years 	ago,
from 	the 	real 	estate industry. They 	have 	a 	marketing
term 	called 	"farming." 	Now. 	when 	you 	hear 	Realtors®
talk 	about 	farming, they 	don't 	mean 	mule, 	pitch 	fork,
funny 	hat, 	sing 	the 	Green 	Acres 	song, 	move to ...
But the 	analogy, 	the agricultural analogy 	is 	there.
What they 	mean 	is getting a 	small, 	carefully 	selected,
manageable target 	market, ·and 	setting 	out 	to 	become
the dominant presence 	in 	that target market 	in 	as short
a 	period 	of 	time 	as 	possible. 	Now, 	they do 	it 	all 	with
manual 	labor, 	of 	which 	I 	do not 	approve. 	But
conceptually, 	you 	just got the best 	marketing 	advice
you'll 	ever get 	as 	long 	as 	you 	live. 	So 	we'U 	spend 	a
couple minutes and dissect 	it.
•
Small, 	carefully 	selected, 	manageable target markets,
set out 	to 	become the dominant presence 	in 	that target
market 	in 	your category of business 	in 	as short a period
of time 	as 	possible. 	It's 	a formula.
Why 	small? 	Biggest marketing mistake most
marketers 	make 	is marketing too 	big. 	rfl 	say, 	"What's
your target market?" 	"Detroit." 	"Well, 	if 	we 	send one
postcard to every 	adult 	who 	lives 	in 	the 	greater Detroit
·area 	once a 	year, 	which 	can 	hardly 	be 	called 	an
intensive campaign, 	what's our 	budget 	got to 	be?"
The guy 	says, 	"$300,000.00." 	c'How 	much 	you 	got?"
"$600.00." 	Problem.
Here's 	part of 	the 	formula. 	You 	must 	som~how
by 	science, 	by 	strategy, 	by 	demographics,' 	by
psychographies, or 	by 	bias, 	but 	somehow 	- 	you
must shrink 	the 	size of your target 	market 	so 	that
whatever resources 	you're 	willing 	and 	able 	to

getting in the 	family 	vehicle, 	schlepping 	all 	the 	way
over 	to 	the 	ocean, getting 	out, 	jumping up 	and 	down
on 	the 	edge 	of 	the 	ocean 	1or five minutes. driving 	home,
thinking 	you 	affected 	ecology 	on 	the 	planet.
What you 	want 	to 	do 	s 	s 	markete 	·s
jump 	up 	and 	down 	in 	carefully 	chosen 	puddles,
not the 	ocean, 	which brings 	us to the second 	thing.
Small, 	carefully 	selected. 	Selection, 	we 	talked
about 	that. 	That's the 	same 	as 	discrimination.
Selection 	is where the battles 	won 	or 	lost 	in 	terms
of leverage. 	Small, 	carefully 	selected, 	manageable
target 	market.
•
Management 	means either they 	are 	all 	in 	the 	same
place, 	or they 	all 	belong to 	the 	same 	thing, or they 	all
read 	the 	same 	thing, or they're 	all 	available 	on 	a 	list 	as
I 	suggested to 	you. 	Somehow, 	they're reachable,
affordably. 	Small, 	carefully 	selected, 	manageable
target 	market. 	set out to 	become 	the dominant
presence in that target market 	in 	as 	short a 	period
of time 	as 	possible. 	And 	we 	don't want to 	do 	it 	with
manual 	labor, 	like the 	Realtors®_ 	We 	have to substitute
something 	for 	the 	manual 	labor.
In 	this particular 	example 	I'm 	going to 	show 	you, 	we
substitute a particular type of direct 	mail. 	Now, 	as
soon 	as l start to 	talk 	about 	direct 	mail, 	one-third 	of
the ears in the 	room 	flop 	right 	over. 	"Man, 	we've 	tried
direct 	mail. 	Doesn't work 	in 	our business. 	Doesn't
work 	in 	our town. 	It's too 	expensive. 	It's 	too
complicated ...
A 	couple 	of things. 	If 	you 	and 	I go through 	life only
doing those things that work 	real, 	real 	well 	for us the
very 	first time 	we 	do 	them, 	there 	will 	be 	less kids 	with
each 	generation.
Secondly. 	you 	have not tried direct 	mail 	the 	way 	I'm
going to 	show 	you 	how 	to 	do 	direct 	mail. 	In 	fact,
everybody makes this mistake 	when 	they 	try
get a list 	from 	somewhere, they print up 	Something,
they send 	it to the 	list. 	and 	almost 	no 	matter what
happens 	-good, 	bad, indifferent or 	ugly- they never
mail 	to these same people 	again. 	Or 	if they 	do, 	it's six
months or a year 	later, 	by 	accident. Let 	me 	save 	you
some 	money. 	Never do a one-shot 	mailing 	again. 	It
cannot possibly 	pay 	off. The odds are insurmountably
stacked 	against 	it. 	Here's 	why.

•
•
People have a 	lot 	of stuff 	going 	on 	in 	their 	lives, 	and
you 	can't control 	timing. 	So 	here's how people don't
live. 	The last thing 	Harvey 	says 	to 	Marge 	in 	the morning
before 	he 	leaves 	the 	house 	is, 	"Marge, 	I 	want 	you 	to
stay home. 	Don't 	go to 	work, 	don't take the 	kids to
school, don't venture 	out, 	don't 	go 	shopping." 	Stay
right here in 	the foyer 	with 	your 	nose 	glued against 	the
window, watching 	the 	street. 	Look 	for 	the 	mail 	man.
Don't 	miss 	him. 	When 	he 	arrives, 	hustle your 	buns
out 	to 	that mailbox. Don't wait a 	minute. 	Get that 	mail.
Because in today's 	mail 	might 	be 	one 	of 	those 	nitty·
letters 	from 	an 	insurance guy offering 	us 	a free 	road
atlas 	if 	we 	let 	him 	come 	over 	and 	beat our brains out
for 2 hours 	in 	the 	evening, 	and 	I want 	a free 	road 	atlas."
This is not 	how 	people 	live. 	They've got other stuff
happening. 	So 	what 	makes 	you 	think you 	can 	print 	up
orte 	crumby 	goldenrod 	flyer, 	send 	it 	out one 	time, 	and
get 	a 	stampede of 	response. 	That 	thing doesn't 	even
knick 	them 	on 	the 	way 	by. 	They 	don't 	even 	feel 	the
breeze. 	. 	tf 	.
/ 	/t;;'\/c_&-
lf 	you 	want 	the 	secret 	ere 	it 	is.__.Kyou 	want 	impact
and 	if 	you 	wan 	response, 	~oomust 	have.repetition.
They 	are 	inextricably 	~:But 	you 	cannot afford
Madison 	Avenue 	kind 	of 	repetition. 	They-call 	it
brand-building. 	Often, 	people 	selling 	you 	advertising
will 	talk 	to 	you about 	this. 	They 	will 	tell 	you 	to 	run 	the
ad, 	run 	the 	ad, 	run 	the 	ad, 	run 	the·ad, 	and 	someday ...
No, 	no, 	no. 	That's 	not 	how 	you 	and 	I 	do 	business.
We 	need 	to· invest 	in 	marketing 	on 	Monday that pays
a 	yield 	on 	Wednesday, 	so 	we 	beat 	the 	check 	we 	wrote
to 	the 	bank 	on 	Thursday. 	That's the kind of marketing
we 	need, 	not 	brand 	building.
So 	we 	have to 	try 	and 	do 	what 	Madison 	Avenue 	does
over a 	long 	period 	of 	time, 	in 	a 	very 	short, 	concise,
intense period of 	time, 	which 	this example 	I'm 	going 	to
show 	you 	uses a particular 	type 	of direct 	mail 	which

Your loving wife 	needs, 	wants 	and 	deserves 	special

at the 	office, 	tired. 	Who 	has the time or the energy to

reason given 	by 	divorcing 	women, 	'He 	just didn't
pay 	enough attention 	to 	me 	anymore. ')" 	I 	wrote this
while 	I 	was watching 	Oprah .
•
Now, 	this two-page 	letter goes on 	to 	further describe
the 	problem 	and 	then describe the 	solution, 	which 	in
this case is a prepackaged evening 	of 	romance, 	one
price, five-course 	meal, 	table 	In 	a special 	section, 	no
kids, view 	of 	the 	water, 	rose 	in a vase 	when 	you 	get
there, 	strolling 	violinist, 	souvenir 	photograph, 	heart-
shaped box 	of 	candy to take 	home. 	That's 	called 	an
offer. 	It's 	useful 	to 	know 	how 	to 	make 	one 	of 	those,
but that's not 	my 	point. 	What's important here is 	what
happens to everybody that gets that 	letter 	and 	does
not respond, does 	not 	book 	reservations? Fifteen
days later, they get 	letter 	number two.

real 	life, 	where the 	circles 	are, 	it has three pennies
glued 	to 	it. 	The 	headline 	says, 	"Three 	coins in 	the

paragraph 	says, 	"You 	see, 	this is your second notice.

Now, 	some 	people 	are 	thinki~g, 	"Wait 	a 	minuter 	I 	sell

Phoenix. 	where 	I 	live. 	There are 	300 	of 	us 	who 	live 	in
Phoenix. 	I 	don, 	know 	why that 	is. 	I 	wish 	299 	of 	them
would 	move. 	but 	that's 	neither 	here 	nor there. 	What's

should, 	it's 	not the 	only thing 	you 	can 	use. 	It's 	not
the 	only thing 	I 	teach 	you 	how 	to 	use. 	But 	done 	right
consistently, 	biggest bang 	for your 	buck. 	If 	you 	want
to 	make 	direct 	mail 	work. 	there's 	two 	things 	to 	write
down 	if 	you 	write 	down 	nothing 	else. 	This is the first.

· 	people 	send 	mail 	under 	the 	assumption 	that 	since
they 	sent 	it, 	people 	will 	open 	it. 	No. 	America 	sorts 	its
mail 	over 	a wastebasket. 	If 	you 	don't 	make 	the 	cut,
nothing 	else 	counts. 	You 	cari 	have 	inside 	the
envelope 	one 	of those 	brochures 	the 	home 	office

college 	on 	it, 	it's 	on 	coated 	stock, 	it's die-cut in 	the

embossed 	with 	the corporate 	logo. 	When 	you 	open
it 	up. 	a 	little 	house pops 	up. 	music 	plays. 	Doesn't
make 	any difference 	if 	they don't 	open 	the 	envelope.

do 	it. 	There are 	others, 	but 	that way's 	pretty 	good..

caught 	them. 	The first is 	called 	"a 	champion·s 	circle
. 	of 	influence." 	Now, every single 	one 	of you has 	at
least 	one, 	probably 	several 	champions. 	These 	are
the customers, the clients, the patients 	who 	love 	you,
who think 	you 	walk 	on 	water, 	who 	believe 	you,ve 	done
something 	extraordinary, 	who 	is always saying to 	you,
"If 	there's anything 	I 	can ever 	do 	for 	you, 	let 	me 	know."
This 	is 	what 	they 	can 	do. 	Every 	champion 	controls
one 	or 	more 	circles of influence 	where 	you 	could 	do
business 	if 	properly 	introduced, 	where 	their 	name 	has
meaning but yours has none. That's the first 	money-
making 	thing.
The second one, the 	plumber 	goes 	back 	to 	the customer
and he says, 	"You 	know, 	when 	I 	was 	here the other 	night,
you 	were very appreciative. 	I 	appreciate 	that. 	What 	you
probably don't 	know. 	didn't think 	about, 	we 	get very few

there 	live 	in Phoenix?" 	"300." 	"Terrific."

up what happened, kind of 	as 	you 	s~d 	it to me as 	I 	left,
but 	now as a letter from 	you 	to those 	300 	people 	who
know you but do not yet know 	me. 	You 	can 	change
anything 	you 	want 	to change, but 	then 	l 	want 	to put it
on 	300 	sheets 	of 	your letterhead. 	Not 	mine, yours.
which 	I'll 	cheerfully 	pay 	for. 	And 	then 	I 	want to put it
in 	300 	of 	your envelopes. 	· Not 	mine, 	yours. 	And 	I
want to have 	it 	sent to those 	300 	people 	who· know
you 	by 	name but 	do 	not 	yet 	know 	me. 	May 	I 	do 	that?"

circle 	of 	influence." 	It's the 	only 	piece of 	mail 	on 	the
planet 	Where 	100°/o 	Of 	them 	get 	opened 	and 	1QQO/o 	Of
them get read.

that's 	wasted, 	isn't 	it? 	How 	many 	people 	think 	it's
notwuted?
You're 	all 	wrong. 	And 	in 	fact. 	it 	will 	make 	you 	money
just to 	get 	this 	right. 	if 	you 	understand 	this. 	See. 	It's 	a
big 	epic 	waste 	if 	he 	stops there. 	Here's why. 	Think
about 	what 	has 	to 	happen 	now for that 	to 	tum 	into
I 	got 	it, 	I 	read 	it. 	AI 	the 	plumber 	sounds 	like
a 	pretty 	good 	guy, 	the 	kind 	of 	plumber 	I'd 	want 	if 	I
needed 	a 	plumber, 	but 	I 	don't 	need 	a 	plumber.
So 	now 	what 	I've 	got 	to 	do 	is 	I've 	got to ... 	I've 	got 	to
go 	down 	to the 	copy 	shop, 	and 	I've 	got 	to 	get 	16 	copies
of that thing 	made. 	Then 	I've 	got to 	get 	16 plastic 	bags

steal 	it 	and 	use 	it. 	One 	of 	the sections is 	so 	close,
you 	can go 	in, 	change a 	word 	here, 	a 	phrase 	there,
use 	it 	as 	if 	it 	was 	built for 	you. 	You'll 	benefit 	from 	all 	of 	it.
There's two other things 	in the 	tool 	kit. 	There 	are 	' ··	.
102.additional exhibits 	I 	did 	not 	do. 	They 	came 	from 	\
people 	like 	you, 	other 	seminars, 	previous 	years, 	took
kits home, got great 	results, 	found 	something 	they
liked, 	adapted 	it, 	used 	it 	in 	their 	business, 	got 	results,
sent 	it 	into 	me. 	Every 	year, 	I 	sift through 	all 	the 	ones
that 	come 	in 	during 	the 	year 	to update the kit. 	In
December 	of 	last 	year, 	I 	went 	through 	over 	2,000
of them to 	pick 	the 	102 	that 	are 	in 	here. 	So 	you 	not
only get 	everything 	I 	built 	for 	you 	to 	use, 	you 	get 	102
examples of what 	people 	in 	diverse businesses 	and
selling 	activities 	have 	done 	with 	this, 	how 	they've
used 	it. 	how they've 	adapted 	it, and 	what 	the 	results 	are .
•
There's 	also 	six 	additional 	audio 	tapes 	in 	the note 	book.

. 	Making 	a 	lot 	of money, 	in 	and 	of itself, 	is

Not 	necessarily 	that 	much 	smarter ..... the 	main 	difference
between 	the 	7-figure 	income 	earner and
the 	minimum 	wage 	clerk at 	the convenience store 	.
is 	·only 	the 	size 	of their 	bank 	account, 	not 	the 	size 	of 	the 	brain.

in 	a 	way 	that 	is 	pleasing 	to 	you, satisfactory 	to 	yon,

that's 	a 	much 	trickier thing 	to 	get done ...... .
. . • . . . and 	a 	very 	small 	percentage 	of

-- 48 of 122 --

OVERIIEAD#l: 	,JOB/EQUITY
•
Most 	people, 	particularly 	those 	in 	sales 	oriented 	fields,
who 	think 	they 	are 	in 	business - 	aren't.
They 	have 	jobs.
For 	some 	people 	this 	is 	perfectly satisfactory.
<dry 	cleaner 	example; 	be 	your 	own 	boss 	gl.ly;
owns business 	but 	functions 	as an 	employee
....then 	business 	owner, 	then 	entrepreneur.>•
•
Biggest issue 	is 	whether 	or 	not 	you 	develop
- 	and .by 	that I do 	not 	necessarily 	mean
what 	you 	might 	someday 	be 	able to sell;
I 	mean 	something 	that produces 	money 	for 	you
rather 	than 	you 	producing 	money.
I,Il make 	the 	oil 	well 	analogy.
quity
Most people 	in 	your 	business 	do 	NOT 	own 	oil 	wells.
They 	are 	oil 	well 	workers. 	<e~end 	analogy>
Here's 	the result: 	if you 	follow 	somebody.
in 	your 	industry, 	somebody 	who 	is 	quote 	successful 	unquote
for 	5, 	10, 	20 	yeat·s, 	you 	find 	they 	are 	not 	d~ing 	·--
appreciably 	different 	after 	20 	years. 	They 	still 	have 	a 	job,
but 	they 	still have 	no 	equity.
They 	got 	smarter, 	they 	do 	things incrementally 	better.
But they're 	still doing 	the 	same 	work.
Yes, 	they 	have some business coming 	to 	them from 	goodwill
and 	reputation 	and 	clientele 	but they 	still get 	up 	everyday
unsure 	of what 	they 	will 	make 	or 	where 	it 	will 	come from,
and 	they 	still 	do 	all 	the 	same 	functions. 	They 	do 	not have 	equity.
:0

..... he's 	still unpredictable income, unknown sources>
take 	people 	from 	this position:
unpredictable income 	from 	unknown sources to 	this 	position:
predictable income 	from 	known sources.
This can 	move 	you 	from 	sales 	JOB 	to sales 	BUSINESS
to 	business 	with 	Equity, 	because
l:Ut; 	EQUff.Y 	IS 	IN 	THE 	SOURCES.
<Where 	equity 	is 	NOT>
Only 	real 	equity 	is 	in 	the sources.
If the sources 	of 	your 	income are unknown 	to 	you
•
-
at 	beginning 	of 	calendar 	year 	or 	month, 	you 	have 	no 	equity.
If 	the 	sources 	are 	known 	to you, controlled 	by 	you,
in 	a 	sense, 	owned 	by 	you, 	then 	you 	have 	equity.
•

Alan: 	Okay. 	Then 	let's 	get 	right 	to 	it. 	Dr. 	Bemue, 	it's 	my 	understanding that you 	joined 	Todd
Smith here 	in 	a business venture involving certain healthcare products, and 	maybe 	we can get
started with a 	quick 	look at your credential.	s 	and 	your explanation 	of this business.
Dr. 	Bern.ue: 	T'm 	board-certified 	in 	family 	medicine 	and 	I'm 	a 	fe11ow 	of the American 	Academy
ofFarnily 	Physicians. 	T'm 	in 	solo 	practice.
About 	20 	months 	ago, 	a 	fiiend 	of mine 	came to 	me 	and 	said that a division 	of the welt-known
company 	Rexall 	was doing some very progressive 	things 	with preventive healthcare, 	and 	he
though 1 ought to take a look at 	it.
Alan: Now, this 	is 	the same Rexall with the nice 	orange 	and 	blue comer drugstore?
•
Dr. Bemue: 	That's 	right. 	And I think Todd's 	going 	to 	tell 	you 	more 	about that 	later. 	But I 	can
tell you that 	I 	was not looking for another 	way 	to earn an income when 	my 	friend mentioned
Rexall to 	me, 	nor 	was 	I looking 	for another 	way 	to 	~pend 	my 	time.
My 	practice 	was growing 	then 	and 	is 	still 	growing today. 	I 	have 	a 	family 	and 	I'm 	busy. 	But it
did 	catch 	my 	attention that 	he 	was talking about Rexall 	and 	preven~ion. 	Great credibility.
Prevention 	is 	the 	wave 	of the future in 	medicine.
• .
I 	did 	have and 	do 	have 	some concerns 	about 	all 	the changes 	in 	healthcare, 	so 	I agreed to keep 	an
open 	mind 	and 	look 	and listen.
Alan: 	Alright, 	and 	what 	did 	you 	find?
Dr. 	Bernue: 	For 	starters, 	I 	can 	tell 	you 	that 	when 	I 	carefully 	researched 	some 	of the 	Rexall
Showcase 	health products, 	Twas 	impressed. 	The 	product 	Bios 	Life 	2, 	for 	example, 	based 	on
patents 	for 	reducing cholesterol 	without 	drugs, 	is 	very 	exciting. 	That 	product 	is 	fiber-based, 	and
the 	role 	of fiber 	in 	reducing cholesterol 	is 	well-established.
Also, anti-oxidants. 	I'm 	sure 	you've 	heard 	about 	the 	importance 	of the 	antioxidant vitamins.
Alan: 	l 	have. •
Dr. 	Bernue: 	Well, Rexall 	is 	doing 	some 	outstanding 	work 	with 	antioxidants. 	The 	indisputable
scientific basis for 	these 	products, their quality> and 	in 	many 	cases 	their uniqueness, really
interested 	me. 	So 	I 	decided 	to 	get involved in 	the 	business and 	catve 	out 	10 	to 	12 	hours 	a 	week
to 	devote 	to 	it. 	I 	use 	the 	products 	personally and 	in 	my 	practice 	in 	a very straightforward way,
and 	that's 	been 	very positive.
They 	help many people. 	The 	patients like them, 	reorder 	them, 	and 	the 	PR 	is 	good .
That, 	however, 	is 	not 	the 	way 	I 	quickly created a significant income. 	I've 	created 	my 	Rexall
Showcase 	business income with a 	very simple 	method. 	I've 	chosen people 	Tknow, 	like, 	respect
and 	think 	are 	sharp, I give 	them 	an audiocassette 	tape 	to 	listen 	to, 	and 	I've 	told them 	I've 	been
introduced 	to 	what Rexall's doing 	with 	preventive healthcare 	as 	well 	as 	an 	innovative 	way 	of
letting 	people 	participate 	in 	a business, that 	I 	was compelled 	by 	what 	I 	heard on 	the 	tape 	and that
I'd 	like 	them 	to 	take 	a listen 	to 	it 	and see 	what they 	think. 	·

Tha~'s 	it. 	It's 	very 	low-key. 	And 	as 	a result 	of that, 	I've 	been able to replace a little over half of
my 	Income from medicine. 	And 	by 	the end 	of the year, I expect to match 	and 	replace 	my 	income
completely. And 	I 	can tell you that from a business standpoint, the only way that you can
succe~d 	in 	a 	company 	like 	this 	is 	by 	helping as 	many 	other people succeed 	as 	possible. 	And 	T
can 	think 	of no better business principle than that 	one. 	.
Alan: 	Well, duplicating the income from 	a 	medical practice that took you years 	of costly
education and years 	of work to build, 	in 	just 	20 	months, 	is 	impressive. But what 	l 	hear regarding
this business sounds 	like 	multi-level 	or 	network marketing. 	And 	quite honestly, 	Tthink a lot 	of 	~
our 	listeners just went 	"Uh-oh" 	when they heard that.
Dr. 	Bernue: 	I'm 	sure you're 	right. 	ButT guess 	I'd 	warn everybody about too quickly closing
their minds. 	As 	a 	matter 	of fact, 	when Tfirst 	heard a tape, Tknew very tittle about network
marketing. And quite frankly, the little I 	did 	know 	I 	didn't really 	like. 	But because I 	had 	respect
for the friend who gave 	me 	the tape, 	I 	decided to 	do 	a 	little investigating into the industry. 	And 	I
found there are many network marketing companies, and they 	all 	do business a little differently.
Rexall 	is 	very upfront about their business. 	I 	tell people there's compelling infonnation 	I 	want
them to listen to. 	On 	the tape, they hear that it's Rexall, that there are breakthrough products 	in
preventive healthcare, and that it's network marketing 	with 	the opportunity to create a pennanent•
income. 	I 	only want to 	talk 	further with those 	who 	want to learn more.
•
Alan: 	Okay, 	Todd, 	let's 	have the pitch.
Todd 	Smith: 	l 	don't want to deliver a sales pitch. 	In 	fact, that's contrary to the 	non-
confrontational way we conduct our business. But let me give you just 	five 	quick 	points to
consider that are largely responsible for people 	of the 	caliber 	of Dr. 	Bemue 	and 	the other doctors
you~11 	meet being 	in 	this 	business, 	and 	probably telling their patients about the products and
advising their peers 	and 	business contacts to look 	at 	this.
(
Alan: 	Okay.
Todd 	Smith: 	Well, 	for one thing, there's the backing 	ofRexa11. 	Rexall 	has 	been, 	as 	many
people know, a leader 	in 	healthcare since 	1903 	as 	well 	as 	a 	leader 	in 	business innovation.
Certainly, most people remember the little corner dn1gstore with the soda fountain. 	Well, 	it was
independently owned. It 	was 	a 	partnership between Rexall 	and the 	hometown phannacist.
Rexall was 	an 	early leader 	in 	the franchising concept. 	And 	today, they're pioneering 	a new and
improved approach to 	multi-level 	marketing, or network marketing, 	as 	well 	as 	the 	virtual 	store.
Alan: 	Okay, 	yoll 	got 	me 	there. 	What 	is 	a 	virtual store?
Todd 	Smith: 	Well, 	instead 	of a physical facility with 	walls, 	shelves, inventory, employees 	and
substantial overhead, our independent distributors utilize catalogs, audio brochures, toll-free
numbers and direct shipments, 	even 	computers 	and 	the internet, to 	bring 	the 'store 	to the
customer.
Alan: 	Okay, 	interesting. 	What 	else?

-- 71 of 122 --

•
ln 	Fe?ruary 	of 1?96, 	in 	the 0
Journa1 	of the American Medical Association, there was a study
shoWlng that a htgh-fiber dtet alone could 	even 	reduce the risk 	of having a heart attack.
Bios 	Li~e 	2 	cont~ins 	the 	mos: 	adv~nced 	matrix 	of beneficial 	fibers) 	vitamins 	and 	minerals to 	help
you 	avotd 	these 	hfe-thr~ate~tng 	dtseases. Personally, I've seen patients with extremely 	high
cholesterol, clearly at 	high 	nsk for heart attacks or strokes, where dietary modifications alone
were not working, prescription drugs were not working, and Bios Life 	2 	brought 	down 	the
cholesterol dramatically. .
Hardly 	a 	week goes by that 	I 	don't read something 	in 	one 	of the 	medical journals about new
studies demonstrating the benefits 	of one 	or 	more 	of the ingredients 	in 	these products.
Alan: 	Let's 	talk about heart disease for 	a 	minute. What can you tell 	me 	about heart disease that
T don't already know?
Todd 	Smith: 	Well, just using the word 	"disease" 	is 	misleading. Let 	me 	explain.
Probably everybody listening knows somebody who's had a 	heart 	attack or stroke at 	a 	very
young age, 	or 	a bypass operation. Four thousand people have heart attacks every 	day, 	and one-
third 	of those experience sudden death 	as 	their 	first·and only symptom. Every 	34 	seconds, 	·
someone dies 	in 	the 	US 	from some 	kind 	of cardiovascular problem. Forty-three percent 	of all
deaths are caused by this killer. 	And 	get 	this, 	the American Heart Association estimates that
orte-half of the 56-million Americans who suffer from cardiovascular problems, which inevitably
become life-threatening, have no idea they have 	this 	degree 	of risk 	and 	are doing nothing about
it.
One 	obstacle to prevention 	is 	this idea 	of heart disease. When you think 	of disease, you think
there's not much you can 	do 	about 	it. 	Maybe it's genetic 	in 	the 	family, 	maybe 	it's stress, 	maybe
it's just bad luck. After 	all, 	that well-known runner, 	Jim 	Fix, 	died 	of a heart attack.
Well, here's 	a fact that you just don't hear enough about. 	You 	can reduce your risk for heart
attacks and strokes by getti.ng 	the proper nutrients 	1n 	your diet. There are 	nutritiona1
supplements, such as Bios 	Life 	2 	and 	Ce11ular 	Essentials, that 	can 	help 	prevent heart attacks,
potentially even reverse existent heart disease without surgery 	and 	without taking 	costly
prescription drugs with dangerous side effects.
You're married 	to 	a beautiful, young, very young wife. 	You 	ought to know about this 	and 	use 	it.
Alan: 	Yes. 	Well, 	I'm 	sure she'd appreciate anything you can do to keep 	me 	alive, 	Dr., 	but 	you

Todd 	Smith: 	Okay, 	Alan, 	let's talk nuts and bolts for a minute. First, you have 	to 	remember
that 	it's 	th~ 	strength and 	credibility 	ofRexall and the unique benefits 	of these products that make
it possible to attract large numbers 	of high-quality people 	in 	this business. Without that, it's
important to understand this would be just another multi-level marketing business where it would
be 	very difficult for people 	to 	succeed. I certainly wouldn't be involved at 	all.
This 	is 	the only kind ofbusiness that really gives ordinary people the power 	of multiplication.
Just 	like 	McDonald's 	has 	thousands 	of locations, or Pepsi, it gets sold 	to 	a million vending
machines, I benefit from the 	sale 	of products 	by 	my 	distributors, who are multiplying. 	And 	there
is 	no 	other business where 	I 	could work from home, have 	no 	big overhead, 	no 	employees, but
have thousands 	of outlets 	or 	virtual stores 	all 	over North America.
Now, as 	my 	business 	is 	maturing, I personally sponsor fewer new distributors and devote more
of my 	time 	to coaching and helping others replicate 	my 	success.
Alan: 	How complicated? How complex 	is 	it 	to make a 	million 	dollars a year?
Todd 	Smith: 	It's 	not complicated at 	all. 	In 	fact, it's quite 	simple. 	Our 	business 	is 	streamlined.
Our 	corporate partner, Rexall, provides 	all 	the research, product, warehousing, shipping,
accounting, computer services 	and 	so 	on. 	We only have very 	few, 	simple things to 	do 	to master,
then to teach 	to 	others. 	In 	contrary to what you 	may 	have observed with network marketing 	in
the past, we're 	not 	going to meetings every 	night. 	We do not manipulate people into coming to
our homes for so-called "opportunity meetings," and we certainly don't knock on doors.
Alan: 	I'm 	hearing things like "simple" 	and 	''streamlined," certainly less complicated 	than I 	had

fight 	heart disease and 	stroke, 	so 	I'm 	very familiar 	with 	this 	concept.
•
According 	to 	the American Heart Association, this year alone, about one 	million 	people 	will 	die
from 	heart disease. 	Over 	one-half of these deaths 	will 	occur 	in 	women. 	Most 	people don't know
it, 	but 	cardiovascular disease 	kills 	one 	of every two women in the 	United 	States 	today. 	And 	a
woman's risk 	of dying from heart disease 	is 	11 	times greater than that 	of dying 	from 	breast
cancer.

evidence 	suggests that about one-third 	of the 	cancer deaths that occur 	in 	the 	US 	each year are

It's this simple: preventing a serious health problem 	is 	better and more appropriate than treating
it after 	it 	occurs.

We're meeting doctors here today. But what 	kind 	of people are best suited 	for 	this business?
!odd 	Smith: 	On~ 	of the interesting things to 	me 	about this business is the diversity 	of people
1nvolved and the dtversity 	of relationships. 	It's 	interesting. The fellow that owns the gas station
in 	town 	bri.ngs 	~is 	cardiologist into the business. 	Tn 	another city, the prominent cardiologist
sponsors hts netghbor, who owns a gas station. •
T certainly don't think there 	is 	any commonality or trend with regard 	to 	age, gender, whether
someone's married or single or whether they've got a special career background or education. .
Our 	products 	are 	attracting 	a lot 	of doctors. But our successful distributors included people from
many 	walks 	of life. 	If there 	is 	commonality, it's philosophical and 	attitudinal. What 	I look 	for
personally in someone 	is 	an 	open, curious 	mind; 	somebody who 	is 	coachable 	and 	somebody who
has good reasons, good motivations to build 	this type 	of business. 	.
That motivation 	may 	be a 	family 	and the desire 	to 	spend more time with that 	family. 	But 	also,
the need to provide a very good income for that 	family.
It 	may be someone who feels that they have more talent potential than they can utilize 	in 	their
present career. 	It's 	often somebody who 	is 	looking to improve their financial situation and 	live 	a
better 	quality 	of life.
Alan: 	What got you involved 	in 	this business?
Todd 	Smith: 	Well, 	Alan, 	unlike 	the doctors, Thad 	no 	experience 	in 	the healthcare 	field . Twas
first attracted by the nature 	of the business itself. 	Or, 	more accurately, Twas dissatisfied with
what I was doing. I was financially successfhl 	in 	my 	real estate business, but totally at the
expense 	of my 	family 	life. 	I was 	up 	early, 	out 	late, 	working most nights and weekends, with 	no
end 	in 	site. My income was totally dependent upon 	me 	and 	my 	efforts; sort 	of what I would refer
to as a 	his:zh 	level 	of manual labor.'-'
As 	you know, 	in 	your business, you get residuals. 	You 	work for a 	hit 	show like 	Growing 	Pains,
vou write music 	to 	create a residual income that's not dependent upon you going to work every.,
day. 	·
I knew that I wanted that kind 	of leverage. 	So 	I chose this business so I could 	mix 	lifestyle,
family, 	freedom, income 	and 	long-teBn security.

with the rat race I was running every 	day 	to stop 	and 	take a look.
company like Rexall was behind this particular opportunity.

super-skeptic.
So 	help 	me 	here. 	What attracted you to this situation?
Dr. 	North·	: 	Alan, 	coronary artery disease 	is 	the number one killer 	of Americans. It 	is 	more
deadly than cancer, street drugs, or 	any 	other cause 	of death you 	can 	name. 	T	n a 	1996 	article 	in
Scienc~ Magazine, two experts predicted that heart disease would soon become the number one
ki11er 	on 	planet Earth.
My 	interest 	in 	Rexall 	Showcase 	began 	when 	I 	reviewed the research data 	on 	the effects 	of the
product Bios 	Life 	2.
~For 	example, in one study, along with equally impressive results 	from 	other studies, the Bios
Life 	2 	product lowered total and 	LDL 	cholesterol 	by 	15°/n 	and 	18% 	respectively, triglicerides 	by
31 o1o> 	and 	increased HDL cholesterol 	by 	30%. 	In 	addition to these outstanding 	results) 	the
cholesterol to HDL ratio 	nonna.lized, 	indicating a powerfhl effect to neutralize overall cardiac
risk.
Alan: 	That last sentence puts 	it 	in 	plainer 	English 	for 	me. 	But to 	clarify, 	are you 	saying 	there 	is
scientific data that proves that 	this 	product reduces 	my 	risk 	of every developing heart disease or
having a stroke?
Dr. 	North: 	I 	absolutely believe that to 	be 	true. 	And, 	it 	is 	the 	only 	non-drug, natural product,
free 	of side 	effects> 	using 	patented technology for lowering cholesterol.

•
Beyond that, very recent data on the beneficial effects 	of antioxidant nutrition 	in 	preventing heart
disease is 	just 	as 	impressive. There' s 	a 1996 study 	that shows there 	may 	be 	as much 	as 	a. 	47%
reduction 	in 	risk thanks to antioxidant supplementation.
An 	even more provocative study was published 	in 	the March 	1997 	issue 	of the Journal 	of
Applied Nutrition, demonstrating that a complex nutritional supplement program virtually
identical 	to 	that found 	in 	Rexall Showcase's cellular essentials, decreased the rate 	of progression
of early coronary disease by 	68% 	in just one year.
•
•
I 	could 	not 	ignore data like this. 	I 	use these Rexall Showcase products with 	many 	patients. 	I
believe 	thc~.t 	we 	are leading the 	way, 	leading the entire approach 	to 	getting the heart disease
epidemic 	in 	America reversed and under control.
•
.
Alan: 	We are learning a lot about heart disease here. What about cancer?
•
Dr. 	North: 	Heart disease is the more fonnidable risk to you. But cancer 	is 	a national disgrace.
For 	example, 	one 	in 	ten American women will get breast cancer. Tfyou have a 	wife, 	two
daughters and two nieces who each have two daughters, 	one-of those women in your life 	is 	likely
to 	say to you, 	"I've 	got 	a 	lump."
Asian 	women, 	however, 	only 	one 	in 	60 	get breast cancer unless they move here and adopt our
diet . •
The difference 	is 	nutrition. 	And 	the primary nutritional distinction 	is 	fiber. Absolute medical
fact: 	high fiber diets reduce the risk 	of breast cancer 	in 	women, prostate cancer 	in 	men.
To quote from the International Journey 	of Epidemiology, ''Population groups with diets high 	in
fiber have a low incidence 	of cancer, 	including prostate 	cancer." 	Yet 	you 	see 	all 	these ads on TV
urging you 	to 	get checkups so prostate cancer can be detected and 	treated> 	as 	if you're going to
get 	it 	and there's nothing you can do about 	it.
.
Todd 	Smith: 	That's right. 	In 	fact, 	Tjust sat next 	to 	a 	doctor, believe 	it 	or 	not, 	on 	an 	airplane,
and 	he said that after 	age 	50, 	you're just about guaranteed 	to 	get prostate cancer.
Dr. 	North: 	Well, he's wrong. With the right kind 	of fiber intake 	and 	nutritional
supplementation, you can drastically reduce your risk 	of getting 	it.
Alan: 	How 	much fiber constitutes a high-fiber diet?
Dr. 	North: 	Tn 	a book 	I 	read recently, 	New 	Facts 	Ahout 	Fiber, 	Dr. Betty Cayman goes into detail
about 	all 	the benefits that one could expect by increasing fiber 	in 	their diet. 	She 	also went into a
long list 	of diseases that could be caused or worsened by inadequate 	fiber~ 	such 	as 	ulcers, high
blood 	pre~sure, 	cancers, the 	list 	goes 	on .
T certainly used to think that I was getting enough 	fiber 	and that fiber was something that only'
the 	e1derly 	worried about because 	of irregularity.
Most experts seem to agree that we need 	25 	to 	40 	grams 	of dietary fiber a 	day 	to give our bodies
a fighting chance against cancer. The average American's 	diet 	has 	12. 	That's a 	10,200 	gram
shortage 	each 	year. 	Try 	running 	your car three quarts 	of oil short 	for 	a 	year and 	see 	what
happens.

benefits) 	such as a lowering 	of their serum cholesterol levels, 	in 	60 	to 	90 	days. 	Your 	immune

've've 	heard, 	the major killers like heart disease and cancer. 	You 	can also lose weight and inches
without 	dieting.
Now 	to 	the products themselves. There are three. Bios Life 	2 	is 	the world's 	only 	nutritional
food product developed from two 	US 	patents for the method and composition for reducing serum
cholesterol levels. According 	to 	the journal 	of the American Medical Association, over 	50
percent 	of all 	adults have cholesterol levels high enough that require medical treatment. 	In 	fact,
there was a 	very 	well-known study called the Framingham Heart 	StUdy, 	that showed for every
one percent reduction 	in 	cholesterol you reduce your. risk 	of heart disease 	by 	two percent. 	In
fact, 	during 	this 	40-year study, not one person 	with 	a 	cholesterol level lower than 	150 	had 	a 	heart
attack.
•
Bios Life 2 has proven its ability to substantially reduce cholesterol levels without dieting or
drugs. 	Two 	daily servings have only 	four 	calories, 	no 	fat, 	but is very 	high 	in 	fiber. •
The second product in the Prescription 	For 	Life program is a whole food antioxidant called
Natt1re Force. 	Note the words 	"whole 	food." 	Antioxidants combat free radicals that damage our
cells and have been 	shown 	to 	cause cancer. 	They protect ourselves somewhat like rust-proofing
protects 	a 	car 	from 	rust, 	but 	it 	has 	become increasingly clearer that synthetic antioxidants made
from 	chemicals 	may not be nearly 	as 	effective as 	whole 	food 	antioxidants..
And the third product 	in 	the Prescription 	For 	Life program 	is 	Optimal 	Perfotrnance. 	This 	is 	a
unique blend 	of vitamins and botanicals 	for 	rnulated 	to 	increase mental alertness, combat stress
and fatigue, increase fat-burning 	metabolism 	and increase stamina.
Everyone 	I know could use 	more 	energy. 	This product is 	fat-free 	and 	calorie-free. 	Tt 	contains
spirulina, bee pollen, royal jelly, 	Siberian 	ginseng, and zinc.
.
And 	the 	final part 	to 	the Prescription 	For 	Life 	program 	is 	our two-fisted product called Cellular
Essentials. You can make your 	ce1lular 	age stay younger than your calendar age, helping 	to
prevent clogged arteries, heart attacks and bypass surgeries. 	And 	the 	secret 	is 	in 	the 	cell.
With 	Cellular 	Essentials, 	we 	deliver five 	different 	for tns 	of vitamin 	C, 	as 	well as heart 	and 	artery
protection 	agents 	like Lysine and 	Proline, 	and the 	much-publicized 	super-nutrient 	Pecnoginal.

~his 	is a 	very_real. business 	and. a very sound method 	of marketing and 	distribution. That's 	why
gtant 	comparues like MCI, 	Spang, 	Colgate-Palmolive, 	Gillette and Rexall 	have 	all 	turned to
network marketing. 	~"~:}-
But 	it's 	important 	to 	understand 	that today's network marketing companies 	are 	very 	different
than 	that 	often 	or 	even 	five years ago. 	Communication 	technologies, 	4irect 	shipment,
sophisticated tools like 	audio 	and video brochures, these 	types 	of things 	have turned 	this 	into 	a
very professional 	business.
Specific 	to 	our 	business? Again, 	we specialize 	in 	non-confrontational 	marketing. That 	means 	no
embarrassment, no rejection, no pressuring people to go 	to 	meetings, no 	sneaky 	approaches,
nothing 	like 	that 	at 	all. 	Our 	business combines the best and 	most 	interesting 	aspects 	of mail
order, direct 	mall, 	direct response advertising and direct marketing 	with 	the unmatched
multiplication power 	of a 	network marketing 	compensation 	plan and 	a teamwork approach .

• •
Only 	by 	deci?ing 	to 	do 	cert~in 	:hings 	on a regular, consistent, day-in, day-out basis, 	can 	you ever
get 	to 	the potnt that markettng 	ts 	as easy 	and 	natural 	and 	pleasant 	for 	you 	as 	is seeing patients.
And that really 	is 	what you want 	to 	get to.
So 	our 	first fundamental, promote on a 	daily 	basis, not every once 	in 	a while.
Our 	second fundamental has to do with a concept called 	"balance 	or 	diversity ~ , 	Most doctors,
when 	I 	meet them for the first time, have only one, two or three methods 	of attracting patients to
the practice working for them simultaneously. 	And 	that is a long way from enough.
•
The reason why that is not enough is that everything has within it 	an 	inherent cycle effect. 	Ifwe
pick any one method you use 	of attracting patients - 	and 	for the sake 	of conversation·, Jet's use
the 	Yell ow 	Pages ad - and we go look at the statistics 	and 	you 	tell 	me 	that last month your
Yellow 	Pages 	ad made the phone ring 	27 	times, we can safely bet the ranch on one 	thing, 	and
one thing only: 	it 	will 	not make it 	ring 	again exactly 	27 	times this month, 	27 	times next month,
and 	27 	times the month after that. 	It 	will 	fluctuate, 	and 	it 	will 	fluctuate 	up 	and it 	wi11 	fluctuate
down.
In some cases and some places, there are obvious explanations for the cycle effect. 	Of course,
here 	in 	Arizona we know one obvious example, which we probably use 	as 	an 	excuse more than
we should, but that has to 	do 	with the fact that a lot 	of people leave here at this time 	of year to go
to cooler climes.
But sometimes, there's no explanation for the cycle effect other than that 	there's·a 	cycle 	effect.
So 	if you. only have one, two or three methods 	of drawing patients to the practice working for
you simultaneously, the odds 	of them 	cycling 	down together 	and 	causing a slump are 	very, 	very
high, great risk 	of that. 	If you have several dozen methods 	of attracting patients to the practice
working for you at the same time, some 	wi11 	cycle 	down 	as 	others 	will 	cycle 	up, 	and 	it 	will 	be
easier to sustain a pattern 	of growth, 	which 	is 	what we want to 	do.
Now, the third fundamental, you're going to like a lot for two reasons. This third 	fundamental
says that within the diversity, we're going to emphasize marketing 	from 	within rather than
external marketing. I 	say 	you 'lllike that a lot for two reasons. Probably 	many 	of you 	have 	what
I call an anti-advertising bias; you 	don't 	like 	commercial advertising for the practice 	and 	would
rather not 	do 	it to 	any 	great degree. 	Also, 	obviously, 	by 	not doing external advertising, 	we 	save•
money. 	And that's 	fine .•
I come from an advertising background. I used to 	own 	an ad 	agency . 	And 	I know how to get
you 	all 	excited about advertising, 	ifthat's_what! 	wante_d_to 	do . 	Tcoul~ 	h~ve 	y?u 	aU _	down 	at the
newspaper tomorrow 	morning, 	or the 	rad1o 	statton, wnttng checks, gnnmng 	wrldfy, 	tfthat was
my 	objective.
But the truth 	of the matter 	is 	most 	doctors, however 	much 	advertising they do, 	could 	afford to 	do
less, not more, because they are not 	yet 	tapping the 	tremendo~s 	potentia] that_	exists to _	grow 	the
practice from 	within; 	where 	y~u 	get your best, _most 	cooperattve, 	least 	Fee-resistant 	patient - 	and
more importantly, your most 	hkely 	to 	refer 	pat1ent . 	•
The way to set up 	an 	endless 	chain 	o; referrals 	is 	with 	referrals. Because the 	pat i en~ 	who 	comes
to you 	as 	a referral believes that that s how everybody gets there, 	unless 	you 	SCfew 	tt 	up 	by

educating 	them 	differently. 	And 	since they 	believe 	that's how everybody gets there, they also
must believe everybody refers 	and, 	therefore, they are supposed to also. 	If we don't mess that
up, 	the referred patient leads to 	an 	endless chain 	of referrals. 	So 	that's the value 	of a referred
patient.
How 	many 	can we get? Every one 	of you 	came in here tonight with 	an 	opinion 	of how good 	a
job you do at creating referrals. Some 	of you 	may 	think you do 	a 	great job. 	Some·of you 	may
think you're about average. 	Some 	of you, 	although you might not want 	to 	stand up and publicly
say 	so, might privately admit that you 	think 	you don't 	do 	a very good job 	at 	all.
Whatever your opinion is, it's probably been 	fotnted 	without a good benchmark to measure
yourself against. 	So 	I 	want to give you the benchmark, 	an 	idea 	ofhow 	many referrals you could
optimally create 	if you really put your 	mind 	to 	it.
•
The statistic comes to us 	from 	one 	of the two 	or 	three trade ...
Excuse 	my 	interruption from here 	in 	our recording studios 	in 	Phoenix. 	•
Our 	time 	is 	limited and this cassette version 	of the seminar, so we'll take 	a 	little leap ahead here
and 	skip over the story behind the referral statistic.
'
... and 	if you go study 	all 	their data for the last 	five 	years on this subject and average it together,
you'll come up with a single number. 	The number is 	52. 	The average adult consumer has
substantial influence over the behavior 	of 52 	other adult consumers.
Now, that hypothetically means that every new patient who came through your practice last year
could have brought you 	52 	more. 	·
It 	is 	at this point 	I 	can begin to make some assumptions about every group 	I'm 	in 	front 	of
The first assumption is there's nobody 	in 	here batting 52. 	If you were, you wouldn't be here.
There are 	many 	more pleasant things you could do on 	an 	evening than listening 	to 	me, 	if you
were averaging 52 referrals a patient.
The second assumption I can make is some 	of you don't know what your batting average 	is.
You 	could guess, but you don't know for sure how 	many 	referrals per patient, per calendar year
you get.
We're not doing an economics seminar tonight, but I suggest to you 	if you want to improve your
profit from your practice, 	that's 	the single, most important statistic you could possibly focus on.
The third assumption I can make 	is 	that most 	of you are 	in 	the mainstream averages 	of your
professions. And the mainstream averages are three 	to 	five referrals per patient, per calendar. 	.
year.
Now, three 	to 	five 	is 	a 	long, long way from 52. We call 	that- 	not to be depressed by 	that- 	we
call 	that the margin 	of opportunity that exists 	to 	grow from within when 	we 	do a lot 	of things 	to
create 	tha.t 	kind 	of growth. 	·
So with that 	kind 	of opportunity available 	to 	us, we're going 	to 	concentrate a lot 	on 	internal
marketing, not external.

'
The 	ne-x:t 	fundamental 	begins to 	tell 	us 	the 	secret 	to getting optimal 	referrals. 	It 	comes to 	us 	fro
Walt 	Disney. 	And 	in 	a second, I'll put 	it 	up 	on 	the 	screen 	exactly the 	way 	Walt first said 	it
which 	is 	almost identical 	to 	the way that 	it 	is 	still taught 	today 	at Disney. But I want you 	t~
know how important the Disney people believe this principle is .
.
If you 	go to work as an 	executive 	at 	the 	Disney 	companies 	tomorrow, part 	of your indoctrinatio
at 	what 	they 	call 	"Disney 	University," 	will be 	an 	eight-hour class doing nothing but-talking about
how 	to 	apply 	this 	principle.
Now, that's 	not 	all 	that unusual, because you'll also spend eight hours in a lot 	of other classes at
Disney University 	wheD: you're first hired, like traditions one, 	to 	make sure you 	know·how 	many
dwarfs 	there are 	and 	what their names are.
But what's unusual about this class 	and 	this principle is 	that 	each year you remain a Disney
executive, 	you 	must again 	go 	to 	that class and spend another eight hours doing nothing but
talking about 	how 	to 	apply this principle.
That 	means 	if you're with 	Disney 	for a 30-year career, 	you'll 	spend 240 hours 	of your life sitting
in a classroom, 	discussing how to 	apply this 	principle. That's 	a 	lot 	of hours, 	and 	that's how
important they 	believe that 	it 	is.
The principle, as Walt said it 	is, 	"Find ways to do what 	you 	do so 	well," 	they've since added
"and 	so 	uniquely," "that people can't resist telling others about you. They don't have to be
prodded, 	they 	don't have 	to 	be 	given 	incentives, they certainly don't have to be paid, they 	feel
compelled to talk about you to lots 	of people."
At Disney, 	if you 	decided you were really bored with practice and 	you 	wanted to go get a 	new
job 	tomorrow, you could 	go 	to 	Disney 	in 	California 	or 	Disney 	in 	Florida 	or 	Disney 	in 	Tokyo 	and
you 	could apply 	for 	a job 	on the 	Disney gum 	patrol. 	If you're hired to work 	on 	the 	Disney 	gum
patrol, 	you'll 	be 	issued a big, wide, 	fat, 	plastic belt. 	And 	on 	one 	side 	of that 	belt 	is 	a 	holster that
holds a pistol 	grip 	spray bottle 	with 	solvent that 	will 	get 	gum 	off of just about any surface.
On 	the 	other 	side 	of the belt 	is 	another holster that holds a radio, a 	wa1kie-talkie. 	And 	your job,
your stationed somewhere 	in 	the 	park, 	out 	of side 	of the guests - they 	call 	their customers guests
-out 	of sight 	of the guests, hidden behind the bushes, the rocks and the trees, 	and 	your job 	is 	to
wait until some other employee, any other employee, spots gum. 	They 	then 	radio 	in 	the location
of the 	gum 	to 	gum 	patrol 	central, which 	in 	turn radio dispatches the 	nearest 	gum 	patrol 	person to
·get 	the 	gum .
•
And at Disney World 	in 	Orlando, 	Florida 	last year, which is larger than the entire 	metropolitan
area 	of San 	Francisco, the computer monitored response time 	of the gum patrol was three and a
half minutes to get 	the 	gum 	anywhere 	in 	the park .• •
Now, 	here's what Twant 	you 	to 	think 	about. 	As 	a pin-headed, pencil-pushing pragmatic
accountant, could 	you possibly justify what must 	be 	a 	mammoth expense 	to 	have 	a gum patrol
that 	can 	get 	gum 	off the 	sidewalk anywhere in 	the 	metropolitan area 	of San 	Francisco in three
and a half minutes 	or 	less, and 	it's 	maintenance? No. 	It's 	outrageously excessive. 	And Disney 	.
doesn't 	view 	it 	as maintenance 	either. 	The 	entire 	expense 	of the Disney gum 	patrol 	never
appears under 	park 	maintenance on 	their 	financial statements, it appears under 	advertising,
because 	that's 	what it 	is. 	Disney 	surveys 	80 	percent 	of the 	first-time visitors 	to their parks 	each
