year. 	And 	th.e~ 	sa~ 	to them, 	o~~ 	of the questions they 	ask 	is, 	"What 	sticks 	in 	your 	mind 	most
~bout 	~our 	VIstt 	wtth us today? 	And the overwhelming majority 	of the people 	say, 	"The
tncredt~le 	c~eanliness 	of the parks." And Disney understands those people go home to whereve
they 	c~rne.rrom, 	tell 	six, seven, eight other 	families, 	who then 	1oad 	money 	in 	their motor homes
and 	bnng 	tt 	to 	the mouse. That's Disney's primary marketing 	system. 	Tt's 	how they 	do 	what
they do so well, that people 	can't 	resist 	telling 	others about 	them. 	. .
lt .makes 	an 	interesting point, 	and the 	point 	is this: 	when 	I 	say the word advertising, most docto
t_	htnk 	of newspaper, 	Yellow 	Pages, Val-Pak 	coupons. 	A very narrow 	definition 	of what
advertising 	is. 	But 	in 	a 	personalized 	profession 	such as 	you 	are 	in, 	everything you 	4o 	or 	fail 	to
do 	falls 	into the category 	of advertising. 	·
So 	we 	want 	to 	redefine what advertising is for us and begin to apply the Disney definition to the
practice 	to 	stimulate retention and referrals.
A 	dentist 	who 	attended a 	weekend 	seminar 	where 	we talk about this at 	some 	length, creative
practice building, came to me at the beginning 	of the seminar 	and 	he said, 	"T 	want you to know
I'm 	here 	representing myself and 	my 	two 	brothers. We're in joint practice together, for your
infortnation, 	in 	a major metropolitan 	city." 	He said, "You've been highly recommended, 	and
this seminar 	may 	be make-or-break-it for 	us. 	Tfwe don't go home 	from 	here 	and 	create 	some
real dramatic growth, we're going to be looking for jobs.''
Here's his 	numbers. 	The 	previous 	year, 	this 	three-dentist 	practice combined total 	gross 	revenue,
under 	$50,000. 	That's 	called a dentist 	in 	trouble, and very unhappy dentists' spouses.
Twenty-four months later, 	12 	months 	to 	grow, 	12 	months to keep score, not a dollar increase 	in
external 	advertising, 	same 	three-dentist practice, 	same 	office, 	combined gross over 	$560,000.
That's 	almost 	a 	12-times increase. Happy dentists, happy dentists' spouses.
How they got from point A 	to 	point B, 	whether 	you want a 12-times 	increase 	or a two-times
increase, 	or 	a half increase, 	how 	they 	got 	from point A to 	point 	B is 	through 	the 	manic
application 	of this principle, 	using a 	technique, 	in 	part, 	called five-sensing, which I'll teach 	you 	a
few minutes, before our 	first 	break.
He 	called 	me 	shortly 	after 	the 	seminar. He said, "I got 	back 	to 	the practice 	and 	I 	made 	a 	list 	of
300 	things to change 	in 	the practice." Now, that sounds like a lot 	of stuff, 	doesn't 	it?
Another 	important 	principle: 	if this 	were a 	plate-glass 	window 	ins~ead 	of a screen, we could take
the 	best 	pitcher 	in 	the major 	leagues, stand 	him 	right 	here, 	this 	close, 	give 	him 	one 	copper 	bee-
bee, and 	he 	couldn't break the 	glass.
But 	we 	could take the worst pitcher in your neighborhood little league, 	put 	him 	all 	the 	way 	at 	the
back 	of the room, 	make 	him 	pitch backwards 	and left-handed, and 	if we gave 	him 	about 	75 	bee-
bees soldered together, he'd shatter the glass into a billion pieces.
Little things, added 	together, have 	mammoth 	impact: 	The_doctor who 	~pends 	his 	time 	!rying 	to
find 	the 	one magic, 	big 	hit 	that will 	ten-times 	a 	practtce, 	wtll 	never 	find 	tt 	because there s 	no
such magic 	yet.
But 	you 	put a lot 	of little things together and 	you 	have the same impact as finding the 	it.

of each 	of the dentists and assistants, and next 	to 	them they put descriptions 	of who they were,
where 	they 	went 	to 	school and grew 	up, 	who their favorite movie stars were, what their favorites
foods were, 	so 	the young patient could 	pick 	his dentist and dental assistant based ·an 	having

Number 	299 	on 	the list 	of300, 	each new patient left the office for the first time with 	an
autographed, eight-by-ten glossy 	of his 	dentist and 	his 	dental assistant.
•
Here's 	what happened 	in 	that community. 	At PTA meetings, coffee breaks, 	lunch 	breaks,
backyard barbecues, everybody had something 	to 	talk about other than the price 	of snow tires.
So 	the 	topic 	of conversation was little Johnny's weird dentist and what was going on 	in 	that
office. 	And through word-of-mouth advertising, which 	is 	the most beneficial 	kind 	of advertising
of all, 	and 	the only kind you 	can't 	buy by 	the column 	inch, you have to 	creatively 	earn 	it, 	the
practice 12-tirnes'd itself
The fifth fundamental kind 	of ties 	to 	that. 	It 	comes out 	of an 	experience Thad a couple 	of years
ago. 	I'd 	done the corporate seminar 	in 	a very 	small 	town 	in 	Ohio: 	I 	was stuck. 	Tcoul~n't 	get 	a
flight out. 	So 	I was sitting in a Holiday Inn at about 	5:30 	on a Fnday afternoon, watching the
cigarette machine blink. And two almost elderly 	couples_ 	came 	in, 	sat 	_dow_n 	together
immediately behind me, and they began to talk about thetr doctor, thetr chtropractor. 	They 	aJI
went 	to 	the same doctor. •

the 	~ent 	kind 	of through a litany 	of their pains and problems and 	~ow 	much 	better 	they 	were

Interesting stat: the American Management Association tells us this about 	consumers· 	that the. 	)
avera?e 	satisfied customer, without any prodding 	or 	provocation, 	will 	tell three people about 	his
expenence.
No~, 	there's another 	level 	above that called the enthusiastic customer, 	in 	your case enthusiastic
pattent, who may 	tell 	52.
The other end 	of the spectrum, the average unhappy consumer 	will 	gripe to 	11 . 	That's almost a
four times ratio 	to 	the negative. 	And 	if you're not careful, 	it 	can set up a negative referral rate
that 	will 	do exactly what it did 	to 	the doctor 	in 	this town. 	It put 	him 	completely out 	of business.
The 	sixth 	and last fundamental is a simple one. 	It 	says that you and 	I 	have to 	find 	some ways to
have 	fun 	in the practice in general, and speciflcatly 	in 	promoting. There's a reason why 	fun 	is
directly connected to your effectiveness in business and in promoting your practice. And the
reason 	is 	this: 	if you and 	I 	come from the womb with any one 	ability, 	I 	believe just about
everything is learned behavior, but 	if we come from the womb with anything we never had to
learn, this 	is 	it. It's the ability to 	find 	a myriad 	of reasons to avoid doing what we 	really don't
want to do. And somewhere in here there's a doctor with incomplete insurance forrns dating
back to before Christ, piled up 	in 	his office.
T have a desk drawer 	in 	my 	office 	in 	pretty much the same condition. 	And 	whoever you are, you
and 	I, each week, we begin the week with that right up top 	on- the list 	of things to do this week,
with the best 	of intentions. But somethlng always seems to come 	up. 	And 	something always
will. 	And if we try 	an4 	get you to promote the practice with methods you must use teeth-gritted,
hating every minute 	of it, 	you 	wi11 	subconsciously, 	if not consciously, defeat the process before 	it
has 	a 	chance 	to 	work.
So 	we've 	got to 	find 	methods- 	and they'll be different for everybody- that you can 	actua11y
•
~ 	~
<j ~~ 	J\
have some fun with 	or 	you simply will not do them.

So 	to 	review real quick, the six fitndamentals that we kind 	of have to agree on, number one, 	is 	to 	......._ 	'\\ 	,_
accept the necessity 	to 	promote on a day-to-day and daily basis, not just once 	in 	a while, not 	~ 	--::1
f'. 	"" 	' 	~when you get nagged into it, and not when you're desperate. 	. 	. 	~ 	~ 	~
~ 	1\'Two it's to strive for balance 	and 	diversity. 	One, 	two or three methods 	of attracting new
'patients are simply not enough, thanks to the cycle effect we .need 	several dozen.
Within that diversity, we want 	to 	emphasize internal marketing. The statistic 	of 52 tells us
there's plenty 	of margin 	of opportunity to do so. ••
Next begins 	to 	te11 	us how 	to 	do that, and that 	is 	that being 	really, 	really, really good 	is 	your best
ad. 	Find ways to do what you do so well 	and 	so uniquely, that your patients can't resist telling
others about you.

I'm intenupting again. 	At 	the actual seminar, we next 	do 	a couple audience-involved workshop-
type activities that just do not translate well onto cassette tape, to demonstrate a few media and
methods for making the six fundamentals come alive 	in 	the practice.
Instead, 	we're 	now going 	to 	jump ahead again, 	this 	time 	to 	the second segment 	of the seminar.
In 	the actual training program 	we'll 	be 	talking about later, we cover 	28 	different w.	ays you can
create practice growth within the criteria we've established. But for now, we'll move on to talk
about advertising and promotion in print.
In just 	a 	second, I'll give you your second audience test 	of the evening. 	On 	the first one, 	by 	the
way, 	so-so. Maybe 	a 	six. 	He 	really brought your score down 	wit, 	the Popular Mechanics.
This 	is 	a 	test 	of your advertising acumen. Most groups do real well 	on 	this.
•
I 	want you 	to 	remember, though, as we go through the test and 	we 	talk about the handful 	of
things afterwards, I 	will 	not keep reminding you 	of this, that each principle we talk about 	applies
to 	anything and everything you might do 	in 	print 	to 	promote your practice. 	It makes 	no
difference whether 	it's 	a 	Yellow 	Pages ad, a 	V al-Pak type coupon, a welcome-to-the-
neighborhood letter, a clinic brochure, newspaper. 	If it appears 	in 	print, these principles apply
and 	will 	help you make it more effective.
Now, the 	test. 	I 	want 	you 	to 	l~okjust 	to headlines. 	Don't try 	and 	read the body copy. Trust 	me,
it's 	the same 	in 	both ads. 	Just look at the headlines.
The first headline, number one, says, "Your home 	is 	often noisy 	as 	a busy 	street." 	The second
headline says, 	"How 	to 	cut down noise 	in 	your kitchen for 	as 	little 	as 	$30."
Here's the question. In a controlled test, 	if, 	in 	fact, one outperfo1 tns 	the other, which 	is 	the more
effective headline? 	How 	many people vote for number 	one, 	up 	top? Raise your hands nice and
proud. 	One? 	One? How many people think number two? 	Okay. 	How many people don't think
at 	a11? 	1 	can tell that, 	T' m up 	here.
You 	did 	marvelously well. 	You 	get about 	a nine.
The second one outperfonns the first one by ten 	to 	one margin in control tests, which 	is
mammoth 	in 	advertising. 	Tt 	is 	more important to know 	~hy 	than 	to 	have guessed 	right. 	And
we'll come back to that 	in 	a 	second. But the first principle to 	p1ug 	into your memory 	is 	this: 	if
vou want print to 	work, 	the place 	to 	do 	your work 	is 	on the headline. 	The 	headline must 	do
~ertain 	things for you, 	or 	nothing else matters. Because the majority 	of people detenriine
whether they are going to go any further with you or not based 	on 	the effectiveness 	of the
headline. 	So 	if we 	don't 	make the headline work, nothing else matters .
.
Now, 	there are three basic types 	of headlines. 	It's 	very easy to master, because there's only three
in 	the whole 	\vorld.
One 	is 	the one that didn't work so 	well. 	That 	is, 	the technical advertising 	terrn 	for that 	is 	a 	"so
what?" 	Your 	home 	is 	often 	as 	noisy 	as 	a 	busy street. The guy says, 	"I 	live here, I already know
that. 	On 	to the next 	issue." 	Not very interesting, not very provocative. 	It 	doesn't work 	very
well, it 	is 	just a statement 	of fact.
-

.•
'
•
· 	Thi~ 	~ype 	of headline, 	wh!ch 	work~ 	extr~mely 	well, works because it telegraphs the promise 	of a
pos1t1ve 	benefit. 	Read this 	and 	we re 	gotng 	to 	find 	out 	how 	to 	do 	something desirable. 	It 	.
telegraphs the promise 	of a positive benefit, and that's what you want your headlines to 	do. 	.
Now, 	1'~1 	give you a little standard 	of comparison, a test 	headHne 	you can 	jot 	down and take
home 	With 	you, 	that you can use to compare 	all 	your headlines - past, present and future -
against, 	and 	you'11 	know instantly whether 	or 	not you've got a 	winner.
The sample comes 	to 	us 	from 	one 	of the most credible media 	in 	America, 	The 	National 	Inquirer.
And 	in 	The 	National 	Inquirer, 	this classified 	ad, 	when a classified ad is nothing more than a
headline and a 	response 	instruction, 	this 	ad has run 	in 	every single 	issue 	of The 	National Inquirer
since longer 	than 	I 	have 	been alive. 	That 	is 	a 	real 	good 	indication 	that 	it 	works.
The ad says this: 	"Corns 	gone in 	five 	days 	or 	money back. Send $X 	to 	Post 	Office 	Box 	3,
Grand 	Central 	Station, 	New 	York, 	NY." 	Here's the headline again. 	"Corns 	gone." 	In 	two
words, 	it 	telegraphs the benefit as concisely as you could possibly say it. "Corns gone.'' 	Tt
strengthens the benefit then with a timeline that makes it more 	believable, 	"In 	five 	days," 	and
strengthens it again with a guarantee, 	"or 	money back," 	all 	in eight words. It stands alone and it
telegraphs 	a positive benefit.
So 	how do we test your headlines? 	It's 	very 	simple. 	We take your headline away, out 	of
context, 	away 	from 	everything else, 	and 	we 	run 	it in 	The 	National Inquirer 	at 	$35 	a 	word, 	and
see 	if anybody would respond on the same basis as that ad.
So 	we 	open the 	Yell ow Pages, we look at your 	ad, 	and 	what 	we 	might 	well 	find 	is 	the headline
is, 	"Dr. John 	Smith." 	So 	in 	The 	National 	Inquirer, 	at 	$3 5 a 	word, 	we run this 	ad. 	"Dr. John
Smith. 	Write for more information, Box Three, Grand 	Central 	Station, New 	Yor~ 	NY." Would
you get a lot 	of letters? Probably not. 	lt 	doesn't work. 	It 	doesn't 	starid alone. 	Tt 	says 	nothing.
Therefore, 	it 	is an ineffective headline. 	·
So 	I 	told 	you there were three types ofheadlines. 	The first, a statement 	of fact. 	You 	do 	not want
to 	use that. The second, promises a positive benefit. You do want to use 	that.
The third type 	of headline!' 	I mention only 	in 	passing because it is the exclusive invention 	of my
friends, the chiropractors. 	The 	dentists have not come up with anything to rival 	this. 	And 	it 	is so
bad, that it actually appears 	in 	three different 	advertising textbooks that 	I 	know 	of as 	an 	example
of what not 	to 	do. 	Yet 	it 	is so 	common, everyone 	will 	recognize 	it 	instantly. 	It 	is 	a one-word
headline, generally accompanied 	by 	a little drawing 	of a 	guy 	bending over with stars coming out
ofhis back. 	And 	the headline 	says, 	"You're 	right, 	pain."
As 	far 	as 	we 	know, 	with the 	exception 	of the City 	of San 	Francisco, 	there 	is 	no 	major market
area in the country where a substantial number 	of people get up every morning 	and 	go out 	in
-
search 	of pain.
Ro1aids 	took the 	number 	one 	product 	position 	away 	in 	the 	antacid 	category 	from 	Tumsl 	after
many years. 	They did it with 	a~ 	advertising campaign that 	you 	all 	know 	by 	heart and that has
caused some kids to flunk spelhng.
The advertising campaign 	was 	initiated 	with 	Super 	Bowl 	televisi~n 	advertising featuring Roger
Staubuck, 	forrner quarterback 	of the 	Dallas 	Cowboys. 	Roger 	said, 	"Rolaids spells gas.'' 	No.

• •
''Rolaids spells relief," because that's what people want. Don't write headlines that p 	·
people what they 	don't 	want. 	That's not very astute. 	romtse
How 	to make print work? We said make the headlines work. Very.important.
Second thing 	you 	can do, which will be a 	surprise 	to 	many 	of you, 	is.to 	increase the 	amount 	f
~opy. 	Most 	advertis~? 	has 	too 	little copy. 	I'm 	going to teach you an advertising technique ~hat
tfyou 	went to. adverttstng. school you would spend a 	semester 	on 	this. 	We'll 	do 	it 	in 	about 	three
mtnutes. 	It 	will 	help you 	tncrease the 	amount 	of copy 	in 	your 	ads 	and 	double the effectiveness at
the same time. 	It 	is called Double Readership 	Path.
lt 	is based 	on 	the 	idea that 	we 	can divide 	consumer behavior 	into 	two 	extremes. 	Gentle1nen 	Jet
me 	see your 	shoes. 	You 	buy your 	own 	shoes? 	Do 	you 	pick 	out your 	own 	shoes? Those 	ar~ 	your
personal preference? 	T don't 	want 	to 	know 	why they are your personal preference.
Do you know 	him·pretty well? Will 	you tell 	me 	the truth about 	him? 	Thank 	you 	very 	much.
At one end 	of the consumer behavior spectrum is a fellow like Dr. Fred, who when he buys a
new 	car, he 	knows 	a tremendous 	amount 	about that car. He has 	really 	studied 	it. 	He has 	maybe
read articles 	in 	consumer reports. 	He 	has talked 	to 	a mechanic or two. 	He 	didn'tjust 	test-drive 	it
once, he test-drove 	it 	two 	or 	three times. 	He knows 	how many miles per gallon it gets going
uphill, north, 	in 	a 	windstorn1. 	He knows a lot 	about 	that 	car.
At 	the opposite 	of the consumer spectrum 	of behavior 	is a 	guy 	like 	~e, 	who 	buys 	a new car
because it 	is 	red. 	That is called the analytical consumer 	and 	the impulsive 	consumer. 	Doctor, 	do
l 	have 	him 	pegged? Thank 	you.
You 	can 	predict adult male behavior with a quadrant system using shoes. That 	is 	a whole other
seminar, 	though. 	But 	nevertheless, you can 	do 	it. 	And you can 	do 	it 	with supreme 	reliability.
What happens 	to 	us amateur ad writers is this: 	if not careful, Dr. Fred 	will 	tend to create 	material
that appeals 	to 	other analyticals at the expense 	of the impulsives. 	If not careful, I 	will 	create
material that appeals to impulsives like me at the expense 	of the analyticals.
you 	want 	to 	do both. And the analytical and the impulsive follow very different readership
paths. 	And you must.have both 	of those paths 	in 	the same 	print~d 	piece. The 	im~ulsiv~, 	me, 	in
eighth-grade American history, 	1 discovered that you 	can 	~ass 	eighth-grade Amencan 	history 	by
reading the chapter titles, the subheads 	and 	the photo capttons. That takes care 	of a C on 	the
•
qutz.

Speaking 	of photo captions, the issue 	of photos and drawings .
•
T really 	enjoy helping 	doctors improve 	the effectiveness 	of their printed advertising 	and
promotion. 	Most 	doctors 	are simply not getting their money's 	worth 	in 	this 	area. 	Some 	are
outright victimized by bad 	advice 	from media representatives 	and 	advertising people with no
sensitivity for the unique factors involved 	in 	attracting response 	from 	prospective patients.
There are 	14 	different points, like 	the two just discussed 	in 	the 	seminar, 	that 	can 	be 	used 	to
strengthen just about anything you 	do 	in 	print to promote 	the 	practice. 	·
Later, 	we,11 	talk 	about 	how 	you, 	too> 	can 	learn and use those keys, 	and 	even 	get 	personalized
assistance 	in 	doing so.
Next, 	I'm 	eager to 	jump 	ahead to the part 	of the seminar, where we introduce a 	very 	important,
very beneficial advertising 	and marketing 	idea, 	top-of-the-consciousness positioning..
The single, most important advertising idea we're going to talk about is a 	thing 	called 	the top-of-
consciousness principle. It 	is 	the answer to 	all 	of your competitive concerns, real 	and 	imaginary,
and it 	is 	a great key to increased retention and increased referrals. I'll 	te11 	you 	four 	quick 	things
about 	it, and then 	we'll 	talk 	about how it applies 	to 	the practice.
The top 	of the consciousness principle 	helps 	us 	with 	the 	idea. 	that it 	is 	more profitable to 	keep
patients than it 	is 	to 	run 	on 	a 	treadmill 	where 	they are 	falling 	out the bottom 	of the 	bucket 	as 	fast
as we 	are pouring them 	in 	the top.
It 	is 	also more 	profitable 	to 	do more 	business 	per patient 	family 	than 	it 	is 	to 	deal 	with 	individual
.•
I
lj
•
•
patients. 	· 	.
Three, some 	of us have learned from expensive divorces, what you take for granted 	you 	lose.
And 	your 	grandmother probably told you, 	"Out 	of sight, 	out 	of mind."
We 	cannot afford 	to 	be 	out 	of mind 	for 	this 	reason: 	your key competition 	today, 	and 	even 	more
so 	tomorrow, is 	not 	the other 	doctor 	in 	this 	room, the 	other 	doctor on 	your street, 	the 	guy 	who
started doing 	all 	the advertising. You compete for consumer dollars, not healthcare dollars. 	And
your competition is everybody 	The 	Hawaii~n 	vacation_, 	the 	colleg: 	education, 	:he 	TRA, 	the 	IRS,

And 	if you want 	to 	know where some 	of your patient .	drop-outs go, they 	go to 	Maui. 	They're 	all
there together. 	It 	would 	be 	a great place to open a practice. 	·
There's 	a 	thing calJed marketing noise. Marketing noise is how we measure what goes 	on 	in 	the
marketplace. Here's a marketing noise statistic 	for 	you.
The 	aver~ge 	adult consumer today is exposed, 	in 	one eight-hour period, to more invitations 	to
spend thetr money than they were in 	thirty 	24-hour periods just 	three 	years ago. That 	is 	a
~ammoth 	increase 	in 	rnarketin~.noise. 	Som~ 	of it is readily evident to 	you. 	For example, what
IS 	the average length 	of a televtston commerctal today? 	Thirty 	seconds, that's correct. What was
it three years 	ago? 	One 	fu11 	minute. What 	will 	it 	be beginning next year, at the 	Super 	Bowl?
Fifteen seconds, that's correct.
In 	a 	five-year period, 	we 	have increased the number 	of commercial messages you 	will 	see 	in 	an
evening 	of television by four times. Last year, 	from 	Halloween 	to 	Thanksgiving, there were 	500
percent more catalogs delivered to mailboxes 	in 	this country than 	from 	Halloween to
Thanksgiving the year before.
When· you go to shop at the supennarket, 	if it hasn't happened around here yet 	it 	soon 	will, 	the
little plastic doomatlog on the shopping cart that holds the vegetables won't have the
superrnarket's name on it anymore, it has little plastic slots into which full-color ads go to 	sell
products. 	And when you stand 	in 	the checkout 	line, 	you will watch a television monitor showing
a videotape selling you products you 	still 	have time 	to 	run back into the store 	and 	buy. 	And 	on
and 	on 	and 	on 	and on.
And 	tomorrow, a new patient will sit across from you in your office and 	sell 	you, because very
often the patient's a better salesperson than the doctor, that he cannot afford the treatment
program you've just suggested to 	him. 	And 	then 	if you 	could be at his house 	on 	Saturday, you
would watch the 	Sears 	truck back in and deliver a 	$3,800 	home entertainment center.
Those are 	our 	concerns. We compete 	with 	everybody for the attention, the interest, the loyalty
and the dollars 	of the patient and the targeted prospective patient. You can't do it head-on,
dollar-for-dollar. 	You 'lllose 	the battle every time. 	So you have to use gorilla warfare. 	And 	one
of those techniques 	is 	called "top 	of the consciousness positioning." 	You '11 	want each patient 	of
yours and each targeted prospective patient to awake 	in 	the morning 	and 	instantly think 	of
spouse, son, daughter, mom, dad, doctor, not necessarily 	in 	that order. 	That's where you want to
be 	positioned.
How can we do that? 	One 	great way to do it 	is 	with 	what we teach ten to 	12 	times 	~ 	~ear, 	a
minimum, not a maximum, ten to 	12 	times a year contact program that 	says 	that a 	mmtmum 	o~
ten 	to 	12 	times a 	year- 	not including, 	in 	addition to their visits to your office for 	care- 	you 	~11
be in communication with every one 	of your patients and every one 	of your targeted prospecttye 	~
patients.

.
And 	she left the office with a handbag 	full 	of clinic brochures and business cards. 	And 	over the

lady who got seasonal greetings.

offish1ng, .un1nterrupted, undtsturbed. Ready 	to 	cast his 	line, 	and 	up 	over the side 	of the boat
comes a 	gtant, 	ugly 	sn~7- 	And 	in_th~ 	snake's mouth, half-swallowed, 	is 	a 	frog. 	Head's 	been
swallowed, 	legs are 	sttcking 	out, 	kicking. I knew 	I'd 	get 	somebody, 	and 	I kind 	of though it 	was
YOl:l. .
· The 	guy 	reacts with instant empathy 	for 	the 	frog. 	Grabs 	the ore, smacks 	the 	snake 	on 	the 	side 	of
the 	head, 	the 	snake 	spits 	the 	frog out, the 	frog 	escapes 	and 	swims 	away, 	still 	alive. 	The 	guy 	feels
great 	for the frog, immediately feels 	bad 	for 	the 	snake.
He's 	an educated individual. 	He 	understands he's just screwed 	up 	the 	food 	chain, 	interfered 	with
the 	entire 	biological process on eart.h, 	has 	deprived this snake 	of a 	meal, 	looks around the 	boat,
has 	not brought any 	food 	with 	him. 	All 	he 	has 	is 	a 	fifth 	of bourbon, 	which 	he 	planned 	to 	pass 	the
day with. 	He 	figures 	it's 	better than nothing, 	pours the 	snake 	a pretty good belt 	of the bourbon,
snake 	swims 	off apparently happy, the guy now feels better. 	Saved 	a 	frog's 	life, 	snake's 	happy,
everything's 	okay.
Not 	two 	minutes 	later, 	that snake,s back 	up 	over 	the 	side 	of the 	boat, 	two 	frogs 	in 	his 	mouth.
That 	tells 	you 	how 	to 	stimulate referrals.

believe are the most important 	of the ideas introduced 	in 	the seminar so 	far. 	?V 	vt-t 	wvl

Let's go back to the seminar now, 	as 	the third 	segment 	is 	getting 	started.
There is 	a.t~ing 	that, _in. 	athletics 	~nd 	sports, is called the inner game. Many pro athietes 	and 	ro
coaches dtvtde what tt 	1~ 	they do mto what they 	call 	the outer game and the inner 	game. 	The~e
are 	so~e 	books. 	There s 	The 	lnner_Gam~_ojTenni~, 	The 	Inner 	Gwne 	ofGol[, 	The 	In 	G,_..
those 	sports, 	you 	should 	certainly get one 	of those 	books. 	·
I visited 	in 	Atlanta Saturday with the assist.ant 	coac? 	?f the Atlanta Hawks, the NBA team, a
~ellow 	by 	the name 	of Brenda~. 	And 	he satd they 	dtVIde 	what 	they 	do 	into an outer game 	and 	an
tnner game. 	The 	outer 	game 	1s 	for 	them, the 	X's 	and 	O's, 	the 	process> 	the 	techniques· 	the
methods, in their case, 	of playing basketball. 	'
The same kind 	of things we have talking about up until now, this evening, the 	X's 	and 	O's, 	how
to 	put together a decent brochure, how 	to 	begin to create stimulus for referrals 	in 	the practice.
The inner game 	is 	what goes on 	in 	our own subconscious system which, to a 	great 	degree,
governs our ability to perfonn the outer game to use what it is that we know. 	And 	so 	we 	have 	to
talk about 	the 	inner 	game 	a 	little 	bit 	if we're 	going 	to 	make 	the 	outer 	game 	work.
The inner game has an interesting aspect to 	it, 	and that 	is 	that in your subconscious 	mind 	there 	is
a war going 	on. 	The war 	going on 	in 	your 	subconscious 	mind 	is 	between 	what 	we 	might
consider the positive 	force- 	your aspirations, your goals, your objectives, things 	you 	would 	like
to 	do, 	the way you would 	like 	to 	be, 	the 	results 	you 	would like to 	get - that 	is 	at war 	with 	what
are called negative influences 	of other people. 	NlOPS. 	Accumulated and present.
They 	have tremendous affect on this. Let 	me 	show 	you 	how this works. 	You 	and 	Tlive 	inside
two boxes. We operate and we function inside two boxes. The 	outer 	box, 	which 	is 	represented
there 	by 	a 	solid 	line, 	consists 	of real 	limits. 	And 	we 	all 	have real 	Hmi~s 	on 	what we can 	do. 	For
example, a 	silly 	example, we 	can't 	go open a satellite 	office 	on Mars tomorrow. It's too 	far 	to
commute, 	the 	little 	green guys don't have 	any 	spines, 	teeth 	or 	insurance.
Way 	inside 	the 	real 	limits, 	though, 	the 	dotted 	line represents 	the 	box 	we 	really 	functi.on 	in. 	~d
that 	is 	the 	box we 	build 	pretty 	much 	for 	ourselves. 	The 	difference between 	the 	two 	ts 	unre~ed
potentiaL 	As 	we learn how to expand the dotted 	lines 	to reach the solid 	lines, 	we 	tap 	on 	realtzed
potential 	and 	we ·create 	growth.
The 	dotted 	lines 	a.re 	manufactured 	with limiting self-beliefs. 	They begin 	to 	be 	manufactured
when 	we 	are 	too 	young 	to 	defend 	ourselves. 	Some psychologists even 	say 	we're 	still 	in 	womb,
but 	certainly while 	we're 	too 	young 	to 	defend ourselves.
A 	good 	way 	to 	demonstrate 	this 	is 	with 	a 	life 	insur~nce 	salesperson. 	I like 	to 	us~ 	them as 	an 	.
xamole because 	their career 	path is very 	short so 	Jt 	doesn't 	take 	much 	of our 	ttme 	to 	talk 	about 	~
~. 	E~er;..body 	in 	here knows somebody 	who 	either 	has 	been 	or 	is 	a 	life 	insurance 	salesperson.
Here,s 	what happens.

They become a 	life 	insurance salesperson. They 	sell 	a policy to their dad 	if he's 	still 	alive 	th 	·
next-door neighbor, their 	doCtor, 	and they're out 	of the life insurance 	bu~iness. 	That's 	calledetr
turnover. 	And the life insurance industry has it 	to 	a mammoth degree, and it puzzles the heck 	t
ofthem. 	ou
What they refuse to understand is this: that life insurance salesperson is reacting today to
somethin? 	that was put into his 	subcons~ious 	mind 	~a~y, 	many years ago. 	Here's -	how your
subconsctous 	system 	works. Whatever 	tt 	hears 	repet1t1vely 	from 	an 	authoritative source it
accepts, 	stores, remembers and uses 	to 	govern your behavior forever more 	unless 	that 	~ieee 	of
programmjng is 	changed 	by 	the 	same 	process. 	'
.
One 	more 	time. Whatever your subconscious 	system 	hears 	repetitively 	from 	an 	authoritative
source, it 	accepts, 	stores and uses 	to govern 	your 	behavior 	forever more, 	unless 	that piece 	of
programming 	is 	changed 	by the 	same process.
So 	when he 	was a 	little, tiny 	life 	insurance salesperson, 	long 	before 	he 	knew 	he 	was going to be
one, well-intentioned people, authoritative sources, parents, grandparents, grabbed a 	hold 	ofhim,
even worse 	if he was a 	she, 	and said 	with 	great 	vehemence, 	"Don't 	talk 	to strangers. 	If you 	talk
to 	strangers, something terrible is going to happen to you. 	See 	this 	picture on the 	milk 	carton?
That could 	be 	you. 	Come 	straight 	home from school, 	come 	straight home 	fi-om 	church, 	don't
talk 	to 	strangers."
He now grows up, he sells a policy to his dad, a policy to 	his 	next-door neighbor, a policy to 	his
doctor, 	and his sales manager 	says 	to 	him, 	"You've 	got to go out and 	talk 	to strangers." 	And 	he
can't.
A practice management friend 	of mine 	says he tells his doctors, 	"What 	you've got to 	do 	is 	go 	out
in 	the community and pretend like 	you're 	running 	for 	mayor. 	Meet, 	greet everybody, 	1et 	them
know who you are and what you 	do." 	He says, "It's 	fun 	to watch the doctors throw themselves
up and say, 	'That's 	not 	very 	professional."' 	And inside, the 	little 	doctor 	throws 	himself up 	and
says, 	''T 	can't do that, 	because 	if Ttalk 	to strangers, 	something 	terrible 	is 	going to 	happen 	to 	me."
There is 	only 	one example 	of a 	million 	possible pieces 	of programming. But 	ifyou 	doubt 	it
exists, 	if it 	seems strange to 	you, 	let 	me 	tell 	you 	an 	interesting stat.
The 	most recent study by the 	American 	Society 	of Training 	and 	Development 	of Fortune 	500
executives 	in 	the 	$50, 000-a-year 	or 	higher income brackets, their number one fear 	in 	life 	they
fear 	greater 	than contracting 	AIDS 	or 	some 	other deadly 	disease, greater than fear 	of heights,
greater than fear 	of loss 	of job, greater than fear ofbankruptcy? Fear 	of standing 	up 	in 	front 	of a
group 	of strangers and talking. 	Absolute terror.
Having done 	this 	100 	times or more a year 	since 	1978, 	I 	can 	tell 	you 	that 	some audiences 	~e.
better than others. 	But so 	far, 	none have been 	lethal. 	I 	can 	say 	to 	you, with absolute 	conviction,
there's 	nothing 	to be 	afraid 	of 	But, 	of course, 	telling 	someone, 	"Don't 	be 	afraid 	of the dark"
doesn't 	make 	them 	unafraid 	of the 	dark, does it? 	-
In 	here 	are 	tapes 	that 	are 	ntnning 	around, many 	of which 	were 	created when 	you 	were 	t_oo 	small ,
to 	defend yourself. 	They 	have been reinforced 	by 	things 	you've 	heard 	since 	then 	and 	thtngs you
say 	about 	yourself 	.

The 	trick is 	to 	get rid 	of those self-imposed limits, to expand them, to stretch them, and 	allow 	us
to 	be 	more 	of what we need to get the success that we want. 	.
Along those lines, I,ve 	prepared 	two 	grocery 	lists I want 	to 	quickly 	show 	you. 	They're 	not 	the
most 	pleasant grocery lists 	to 	look 	at 	in 	the 	world .
•
One 	is 	a 	list 	of the attitudinal inner game, 	if you 	wish, 	characteristics we 	find 	in 	common 	in 	some
of the most successful doctors in the 	country. 	The 	other 	is 	a 	list 	of almost opposite attitudinal
characteristics, we 	find 	in common 	in 	what I call the mediocre majority, who continue to
struggle just 	to 	stay even.
'
And 	by 	the way, 	your 	professions 	are no 	different than just 	about 	any 	other isolated professional
group. 	When 	you look 	at 	any one 	isolated professional 	group and you 	look at 	money 	for a
moment 	as a standard 	of measurement, 	you 	will 	find 	that 	five 	percent 	ofthe 	people 	in 	that
profession 	got 	95 	percent 	of the money. 	There's a 	five 	percent hugely-successful 	group 	and 	a
big, mediocre majority 	struggling 	just to stay even .
•
These 	are 	some 	of the differences 	between 	the two.
One 	is 	that those who 	struggle avoid 	specifics; specific 	goals, 	specific measurements. 	They
choose not 	to 	plan.
Thad a doctor 	te11 	me 	one 	time, 	he said, 	''You 	know, 	I 	don't need 	to 	hear any 	of that 	goal-setting
stuff 	All 	I've 	got to 	do 	is 	show 	up 	at 	the 	office each morning 	and 	the staff runs patients 	at 	m~
all 	day 	long." I said, 	"Gee, 	that's 	terrific! 	It's 	wonderful to have 	such 	a great staff 	Why 	don 	t
you 	put 	in 	a 	bell 	they 	can 	ring 	when 	it's 	time 	for 	you 	to go 	to 	the 	bathroom and 	go 	to 	lunch, 	too,
and 	then vou 	won't have 	to think at 	all?"J
What 	a 	horrible 	waste 	of professional talent 	and education. 	You 	have 	got 	to be 	involved 	in
planning and setting goals and approaching things 	in 	an 	organized 	manner. 	W,e 	find 	th~t 	the
losers 	are 	so 	defensive, that 	for 	them to admit there 	is 	room. for improvement 	1s 	to 	admtt
inadequacy. 	And 	that 	is 	a 	very 	limiting 	way 	to 	go 	through 	hfe.

•
0r 	~5 	years counts. for a lot. 	Tn 	the 	entrepreneu~al 	environ~ent 	in 	which 	we 	live, 	whether 	you
like 	1t 	or 	not, 	showmg up counts 	for 	next to nothing. 	Showtng 	up and being productive counts
for a lot.
Productivity is the use 	of your 	time, 	talent, 	energy, 	resources 	and 	intelligence 	in 	a 	manner
calculated to 	move 	you 	closer 	to 	measurable 	goals. 	And 	how 	much 	of your time you use 	in 	a
productive manner detennines how welt you do. 	·
The responsibility 	issue gives me 	a 	chance 	to tell 	you 	a 	little story 	about 	me.
The 	very 	first seminar 	I 	ever went 	to, 	where this 	kind 	of stuff was 	talked 	about there were 	about
300 	folks 	in 	the room. 	I 	sat 	in 	the back row, by the door, 	so 	I 	could get out. 	The 	guy 	in 	front 	is
one 	of these 	guys 	that 	1ikes 	to bang on the 	podium 	and orate. 	And 	he started the seminar and 	T
thought 	he was 	talking just 	to me 	because 	I 	was 	guilty, 	he 	started 	the 	seminar 	by 	banging 'on 	the
podium and 	repetitiyely 	saying, 	in 	a very booming voice, ''You 	are exactly 	where you want to
be."
I had driven 	to 	that seminar in a 	1960 	Chevrolet 	Impala, 	and 	it 	was not 	1960. 	When 	it 	rained, 	as
it 	does 	366 	days 	out 	of the 	year 	in Ohio, 	the 	car 	leaked from 	the 	top 	to 	the 	bottom 	and 	both 	sides
simultaneously. It 	was 	like 	going through 	a car 	wash 	inside-out. 	·
I had the car for two winters. 	The seats never 	dried 	out in that car, but they 	did 	kind 	of freeze
over 	in 	the 	winter 	and 	you 	sat on a glaze. I
Tfyou know anything about cars, the car had no 	motor mounts. 	The engine just sat there, 	which
is 	pretty 	much okay 	unless 	you 	go 	around a 	comer 	a 	little too 	fast 	and give it too much gas, and
then 	one 	side 	of that engine kind 	of comes 	up and it hangs up against the inside 	of the hood and
it makes a god-awful grinding noise. 	And 	it 	sends the accelerator right to the floor and the car•
into a speed that is in no condition 	to 	endure.
The 	only 	way 	I ever found 	to 	regain 	control 	was dropping it into neutral and letting the engine
bounce back into place, which is not real good for the transmission.
Ifyou 	remember, 	by 	any 	chance, the 	1960 	Chevrolet Impala's, 	they 	had huge rear ends with
huge, beautiful 	fins. 	This car, the frame was cracked on both sides, slightly behind the rear tires.
So 	the 	rear end was held up 	with 	bailing wire, 	wood 	blocks and bolts through the 	floor 	of the
trunk. 	Tf either one 	of the rear tires went flat, there was 	no 	way 	to change 	it 	because there was
nothing 	to 	attach a jack 	to, 	except rust.
No 	shame 	for 	the 	car. 	Tbought 	it 	for 	$25 	on 	six 	monthly payments. 	Tt 	was 	all 	I 	could afford 	at
the time. 	And 	the condition 	of the car pretty much described some other things that were 	gqing
on in 	my 	life at the time. 	And 	I sat in the back 	of the room and I heard 	this 	heathen 	idiot 	say,
"You 	are exactly where you want to 	be." 	And 	I 	looked down at 	my 	lap and I saw 	my 	hands
clenchine 	around 	his 	neck. 	If there hadn't been 	300 	witnesses, the 	guy 	would be dead today.'-"
It took me a 	long 	time to understand exactly what he was talking about. We're 	mends 	today, 	by
the 	way. 	We vacationed together 	in 	the Bahamas and 	I 	haven't held 	him 	under. 	You 	get 	points·
for 	that 	kind 	of stuff. 	·

. very ttme we 	sa~ 	an 	:'i;'s 	the," it's the location, it's the climate, it's the season, it's.the stupid
msurance 	comparues, 	1t 	s the guy who opened 	up 	down 	the 	street 	doing 	all 	the advertising 	it' 8
Barbara 	o~ 	the 	~taffwho 	doesn't 	do her job, 	it's 	my 	spouse, 	every 	time 	we do an ''it's 	the,:' 	we
do 	two thtngs stmultaneously. We take a pretty good sized box 	of responsibility and we push
hat away 	from 	ourselves, and that makes us temporarily 	feel 	good. Whenever we place and
avoid blame, 	we 	temporarily feel good.
However, super-glued 	to 	the box 	of responsibility is 	an 	equivalent size box 	of control that goes 	··
with 	it. 	And 	if we 	do 	very 	much 	of that at 	all, 	pretty soon things really are out 	of control.
Successful people, successful doctors accept 	11 0 	percent responsibility for where they are, where
they want to be. 	And 	if the two 	don't 	match, they're doing productive things to bring them
together.
Last 	on 	the 	list, 	if you 	go 	back 	to 	your 	office and 	you 	say, 	"We're 	going to have 	weekly 	sta~
meetings," 	and the staff says, 	"Rrr," 	there's a reason for that. You're 	boring. 	And 	if you're
boring, 	it's 	a sign that you are bored and 	you 	have succumbed to routine.
There is 	a. 	thing about enthusiasm that cannot be explained on a logical 	level. 	I 	don't like 	dealing
with things that can't be explained on a logical level, but nonetheless we 	will 	deal with this 	one.
If you come, as you should, as you must, to the practice each morning with genuine, honest
enthusiasm for who you are, the service you render, the direction the practice 	is 	going, 	and 	the
direct your career and your staff's careers are going, you somehow imbue that practice 	and 	make
it a living, attractive thing.
If you come without that enthusiasm and are bored and boring, you take the life out 	of the
practice. 	And not only doesn't it attract, it repels other livin 	t 	· 	_ 	~'F- 	) 	P~
When you come to grips with 	all 	that stuff, you 	wi 	face three interesting problems. 	..._, 	·	J?? CPJL'-"'~
- 	. 	. 	. 	. . 	) 	~'-"'~
that 	breed 	enthusiasm. 	Tf you have 	a 	lot 	of ideas) 	you 	don't 	have 	trouble 	getting 	starte? 	tn 	the
morning, you have trouble 	getting 	stopped at night.
• 	•
Two, 	we 	could 	all - 1 include 	myself in 	the 	group 	- 	afford 	to 	hire 	somebody 	to 	walk around
behind us, kick 	us 	in 	the 	rear 	end 	from 	time 	to 	time 	to 	get 	us 	to do 	what 	we 	alrea?y 	know 	we
should do, but 	we wouldn't 	like 	writing 	the checks 	every 	Friday. 	So 	we 	need 	a 	dtfferent 	way 	to
get 	what I 	call 	positive 	nagging.
Th 	is 	a 	little 	thing that has 	to 	do - it affects everything 	we've 	talked about 	tonight- 	with 	how
th:~~man 	mind 	works. 	And 	the answer 	is 	it 	doesn't work 	very 	well . Twenty-four hours 	from
Practice 	Promotion 	Seminar Presentation.doc Page 	20
•
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 107 of 122 --

••
now, you 	will 	have 	forgotten at least one-fourth 	of everything I said. 	And 	more importantly,
everything 	you thought 	of as 	a result 	of anything I said. Four 	days 	from 	now, 	85 	percent 	will
have 	been forgotten. 	And 	16 	days 	from now, you won't even remember having been 	here, 	let
alone 	anything that 	we talked 	about.
The human 	mind 	operates just as your 	mother accused 	you 	of, 	"in 	one ear and out the 	other."
With 	as much stuff as we're exposed to 	today, 	it's 	probably good 	that it 	does. 	If you
indiscriminately 	stored 	everything, 	your 	head 	would 	explode. 	It 	bothers people 	in 	good
restaurants.
So 	unless we interrupt the process "in the 	ear, 	out ·the 	ear," 	there,s only two ways 	to interrupt 	the
process, 	two 	methods, 	if you 	will, 	of doing 	what 	the 	computer 	people 	call 	programming. You've
used 	both 	of them 	all 	your 	life. 	The 	first one, 	you 	probably discovered 	had 	something 	to 	do 	with
the burner 	on 	a hot 	stove. · 	And 	six 	months 	later, 	you.didn't 	make 	the same 	mistake and 	say, 	''Oh
yeah, forgot." 	No, 	the first time was sufficient. 	That's 	called instant, permanent 	programming.
It's 	very 	efficient. But 	when 	we're 	young, 	it 	tends 	to 	be 	painful. 	And 	as we grow 	older, 	it
continues to 	be 	painful and 	gets 	progressively 	more 	expensive. The most expensive 	way 	to
program 	in 	the 	world 	is 	with 	your own experience. 	.
The 	only 	other 	way 	to 	program 	is 	with 	what's 	called 	repetition. 	In 	fact, 	your subconscious 	mind
requires seven 	to 	21 	repetitions 	in 	a 	condensed 	period 	of time 	to get any new 	anything; 	a 	new
language, a 	new 	attitude, a 	new 	aptitude, a 	new 	phrase, 	anything.
Live 	in 	a house 	for 	an 	extended 	period 	of time, 	move to 	a 	new 	home. 	Get 	up 	in 	the 	middle 	of the
night to 	go 	to 	the bathroom, 	your hand 	will 	automatically go 	where? To 	where 	the 	switches used
to 	be 	in 	the 	old 	house, 	not to where they 	are 	in the 	new 	house. 	You'll 	play 	grope 	in 	the 	dark 	for
about three 	weeks 	before 	the 	subconscious 	system adjusts to the 	new 	location 	of the 	switches ...
Want 	to 	amuse the 	staff? 	Doctors, go back 	to 	the 	office 	tomorrow, 	move the wastebasket 	from
where 	it 	is 	to the other 	side 	of the desk. 	You 'II throw paper on the floor for 	about 	three 	weeks,
like a bumbling idiot. 	lt 	takes 	the 	subconscious 	system that 	1ong 	to 	get 	it.
If that's 	true 	with 	relatively mundane, 	unimportant 	things, 	it 	is 	true 	with 	those 	things that are
very 	important 	to 	us 	in 	being 	the people we 	want 	to 	be 	and getting 	the things that we 	want.
Knowing 	that 	you 	need 	repetition, 	you 	have 	some 	limited 	choices. You 	can 	attend 	the 	same
seminar 	over 	and 	over 	and 	over 	again. 	We 	could 	just 	stay 	here 	for 	the 	next 	20 	days, 	and 	I 	could
repeat it every 	three 	hours . I, frankly, 	would 	not 	enjoy 	that 	very much. 	And 	you, frankly, would
not 	enjoy 	that 	very 	much either. 	In fact, 	the 	mind 	can 	only absorb 	what 	the 	butt 	will 	endure.
And 	we 	are 	about 	20 	minutes 	away 	from 	that, 	and we've 	only 	done 	the 	seminar once.
So 	seminars 	are 	really 	not 	the 	total 	answer . 	We 	could 	get 	it 	from 	books. 	The 	average adult
American, 	last 	year, 	read 	one 	book 	once. 	We 	are not going 	to 	tead 	the 	battle with 	books. 	The
way 	to 	get 	it 	best, 	the 	way 	to 	make 	repetition work for 	you, which 	brings us 	to 	the 	inevitable
commercial, is with a device called 	the 	audiocassette. Used properly, 	it 	is 	what 	we 	can a
repetition learning system. 	And 	if functions with 	the 	predictability and efficiency 	of a true
system. The 	audio 	cassette, used 	properly, 	gives us three great benefits, the most interesting one 	·
of which 	is 	true 	automatic learning. 	·
Practice Promotion Seminar Presentation.doc 	Page 	21
•
www.getwsodownload.com	www.getwsodownload.com

-- 108 of 122 --

Example. 	You can actually drive to your office 	in 	the morning, the radio tuned 	to 	your favorite
talk show or music, whatever you nonnally listen to on the radio. Next to you on the car seat 	is 	a
cassette player playing 	an 	infortnational 	or 	educational cassette 	of your choice, volume 	on 	the
cassette turned down 	so 	low 	you can barely hear 	it. 	Pay attention to 	the 	radio, bebop along 	with
the music. 	Do 	it 	14, 	20 	days 	in 	a 	row; 	85 	percent 	of the message on the cassette 	will 	have
pem1eated your subconscious mind and 	will 	affect your speech and behavior not only with 	no
effort 	on 	your 	part, but 	in 	spite 	of you 	entirely.
We know that, 	if from 	no 	other 	way, 	than 	from 	the advertising business 	from 	whence I 	come.
Advertising has used repetition since the beginning 	of advertising and the beginning 	of time.
And you, for example, would pass a test. You would get a ten on a 	test 	ofMcDonald's products.
Tcou14 	stand up 	here 	and hold up 	each 	McDonald's product and you could name exactly what 	it
was. 	Some 	of you would know 	the 	jing1es. 	Some 	of you might 	even 	know the prices. 	Some 	of
you 	would know McDonald's commercials for that 	product; 	not only the current commercials
but the ones before those and the ones before those, 	all 	of which does you 	no 	good whatsoever
unless 	you work at McDonald's.
Every 	one 	ofyou 	knows 	the 	lyrics 	to 	songs you 	don't 	like. 	And 	every. one 	ofyou 	knows
commercial slogans and advertising slogans that are as out-of-date as fashions from 	1950. 	They
got there through repetition. 	· 	·
We'll try 	a 	couple. 	Is there anybody 	in 	here who 	can 	complete, 	"Pepsi-Cola 	hits the 	what?"
Spot? 	Can 	anybody do the next 	line?
Attendee: Two full glasses, that's a 	lot.
Dan: 	Yeah, 	or 	12 	fhll 	ounces. 	Two 	full 	glasses, 	that's 	a lot. 	That's good. That's 	wonderful.
Let's 	try 	this. You stay out 	of it 	now, 	because you obviously are a pro at this. Let's see 	if
anybody else can do this one. Lucky 	Strike 	means .. 	.? 	Anybody? 	1 know you know. 	It's
incredible. Do you know? Lucky 	Strike 	means ... ? 	Close. 	Lucky 	Strike 	means 	fine 	tobacco.
LS:rv.IFT.
Do you happen to know how long it's been since either one 	of those things has been used 	in
commercial advertising?

commercial advertising. 	It 	happens 	in 	the womb. Isn't that 	a. 	long ttme to remember something?

There are two ways you can respond 	to 	all 	of this. 	You 	can choose 	inaction, 	choose to 	do
nothing. 	Toss this tape aside and forget 	~bout 	it. 	And 	that's 	certainly your 	privilege. 	Doing

you have to spend some money. But you still take 	no 	risk. 	Everything 	is 	covered 	by 	our 	90-day

throughout North America. Consider our cafeteria approach that lets you pick and choose the
ideas, media and methods that suit your personality and objectives. 	And 	consider how 	little 	it
really takes 	to 	recover this entire investment. 	Just one 	or 	two 	more new patients than you'd
otherwise 	obtain, 	and 	you're 	home free.

ake 	A 	$3000 	Profit
From 	A $399 	Investment
Len 	Shiken: 	Hi, 	T'm 	Len 	Shiken, 	president 	of US 	Gold 	Chain 	Manufacturing 	Company. 	Twant
to thank you and congratulate you on responding either to one 	of our magazine advertisements or
on 	our television program.
First, thanks. 	I 	appreciate the opportunity 	of introducing our great business to 	you.
Second, congratulations on having the ambition 	and 	initiative to do something positive, to make
life better for yourself and your 	family. 	·
I've made this cassette tape so 	I 	can 	personally explain the opportunity 	that's 	available to you
with my company. Last year, the people I helped get started in the Gold 	By 	The Inch business
sold 	wen 	over $20-million worth 	of our products. 	Some 	of these people have been with us for
years, some have just gotten started. 	Many 	consistently earn 	$50 	to 	$1 00 	or more per hour
working whenever they wish, with no 	office 	or 	storefront, 	no 	overhead, 	no 	big investment, no
risk. •
As 	you can 	see, 	this 	is 	a proven, 	real 	business, not some idea somebody dreamt up yesterday. 	Tn
fact, I believe the Gold By The Inch business is the best thing 	you 	could possibly 	do 	to earn a
really exceptional income part-time or 	full-time.
You 	can learn about the details 	of my 	business 	in 	the enclosed letter 	from 	me. 	That's the one
with the picture 	of me 	and 	my 	wife 	Sandy 	on 	the 	front 	page.
Right now, rather than hearing 	it 	a11 	from 	me, 	Twant you to hear what other people just like you
have 	to 	say about their experiences 	in 	this business.
First, here's Ralph Burden and 	his 	wife 	Janice 	from Washington, who have sold Gold By The
Inch for 	fiv·e 	years. Ralph?
Ralph 	Burden: 	Well, 	like Len 	said, 	Janice 	and I have been selling 	US 	Gold products for about
five 	years now. We started out with their basic starter kit, selling 	12 	styles 	of gold 	by 	the 	inch,
and paid just 	$3 99 for the whole 	deal.
The first weekend, we went to a 	swap 	meet 	and sold over 	$600. 	More than enough to pay for the
complete 	kit 	and our expenses, 	and 	still 	leave some profit. I'll never forget the first 	day 	because
1 made the first sale 	as 	I was setting up the display about 	8:00 	in 	the morning. 	By 	the time the
month was over, we 	had 	sold 	over 	$2,000 	and 	had 	to reorder some more 	chain. 	There was
enough profit the first month to 	buy 	the second starter 	kit 	for 	$399 	and 	channs for 	$350.
.Janice 	Burden: That was one 	time 	when 	Ralph 	was more conservative than I 	was. 	Twanted
him 	to 	buy the rest 	of the chain, charms, earrings and bracelets the first week, but Ralph wanted
to 	make sure that he 	cou1d 	do that good 	every 	week. •
•
-	'

Ralph 	Burden: 	As 	it 	happens, 	Jan·	ice 	was right. 	I 	should have bought the second starter kit
s~oner. 	Because after 	I 	finally 	did buy 	it, 	it looked like 	I 	had 	a 	full-fledged jewelry store every
ttme 	I 	set up.
~y 	the 	way, 	T eventually did buy 	all 	their displays too. 	Tstarted selling Gold 	By 	The 	Inch 	part-
ttme. 	And 	as 	I 	grew in the business, 	I 	switched over to full-time. 	I 	now set up 	in 	malls, 	as 	well
as swap meets.
Len 	Shiken: 	Now, 	Twant you to notice that Ralph and Janice started out with just the basic
starter 	kit 	for 	$3 99, and 	they 	got 	all 	their money back, plus profit, their very first weekend. 	Can
you 	think 	of another business where you can get your entire investment back in 	a 	few 	days 	and
keep making profits from then on? .
That's typical with Gold-By-The-Inch. 	Ifyou 	look at page two 	ofyour 	color catalog for a

this 	kit: 	Tt 	comes 	with 	everything 	you 	need, even the tools to put together the jewelry. 	All 	you 	~ 7

Now, let's hear from Bill Herschel from lllinois. Bill sells Gold 	By 	The Inch strictly part-time.
Bill? 	•
Bill 	Herschel: 	Thanks, Len. I started with 	OS 	Gold about two years ago. I bought their two
Gold 	By 	The Inch starter kits and their 	200-charrn 	display for 	$1,049, 	and 	got the earring kit free.
As 	it 	happened, I had seen it around before and had talked with people 	who 	were making money
selling it at swap meets, in stores and 	malls. 	I answered an ad, got their catalog 	and 	other.• ••
literature, 	and 	ordered. 	·
The first weekend 	Tworked, 	T sold a chann to about every third person who bought a 	chain. 	It
was 	a natural add-on and increased 	my 	sale. I made enough that first weekend to pay for 	my
total investment, have money left over, and still had plenty 	of merchandise left to 	sell, 	which was
now 	all 	paid for.
I marked up the gold chain between ten 	and 	15 	times cost. It didn't take too 	many 	sales to have a•
good weekend. •
After a while, I bought their filigree and fashion rings, as 	well 	as their bracelets. 	My 	display
looks 	like 	the cover 	of the catalog and attracts a lot 	of people.
Tthink the biggest reason that people stop and 	buy 	is 	that they are attracted 	by 	these 	Go1d 	By 	The
Inch displays. Then they discover that 	T can 	make a 	chain 	to fit them, including just the right
size for 	a. 	bracelet. Since everyone has a different size wrist and store-bought bracelets usually
come only 	in 	one size, which 	is 	usually 	too 	big 	or 	too 	small, 	I really become their own jeweler.
I'm 	kind 	of old-fashioned. 	My 	wife doesn't work, and selling 	US 	Gold' s products bring 	in 	.
plenty 	of extra money that a 	family 	needs today. 	As 	a matter 	o~fact, 	my 	wife 	and 	d~ughter .help
me 	out on the weekends, 	selling. 	It's not really work for her, stnce she 	has 	a good tune 	sellmg
and making 	up 	the jewelry.

Len 	Shiken: 	Isn't that a fantastic story? Bill started with three kits: the Gold By The Inch Kit
# 1 and #2, and the charm kit, a total investment 	of $1049. 	And he, too, recovered 	his 	entire
investment the first weekend, plus profits, plus plenty 	of inventory left over to earn thousands 	of
dollars more.
I believe it's really good to start this 	way, 	if you can, because your display looks better 	and 	more
interesting than it does 	with 	just the 	$399 	starter 	kit. 	And 	the bigger, better 	and 	more interesting
your booth and merchandise assortment looks, the more customers you attract.
However, any way you start 	is 	okay. The important thing 	is 	to 	start .
Another great example 	is 	the 	suc~ess 	story 	of Randy Gershaw 	from 	Arizona. 	Randy 	is 	an
engineer by training, with a very good job 	in 	a large corporation.
At 	age 	27, 	Randy 	is 	paying off a divorce settlement, so 	he 	found himself in 	need 	of some extra
income. Here's Randy.
Randy Gershaw: 	· I saw a 	TV 	program for 	US 	Gold Chains Gold 	By 	The Inch business. I sent
away for a catalog 	and 	received 	it 	in 	just a few 	days. 	Whenever I've sent away 	for 	anything
before, I've always had to 	wait 	a month or more. 	Tknew I was dealing 	with 	a good company that
wanted 	my 	business and would help 	me 	get started, because they spent the 	money 	to get 	me 	the
infonnation so fast. 	To 	me, 	that was a good indication that 	if I ordered 	from 	them, 	I'd 	get 	my
merchandise when they 	said 	I'd 	receive 	it.
Well, 	I did order. I ordered five kits 	and 	got two more free. I spent about 	$1,700 	and 	figured
that 	ifl 	could make 	$13,000 	from 	that 	$1,700, 	that was a smoking 	deal.
Two days later, 	T had the merchandise, 	sales 	tools 	inc1uding 	audio 	and 	videotape, merchandise,
how-to materials, everything possible to 	help 	me 	sell . There was nothing left to 	chance. 	They
even had a great flipchart to present to 	merchants to 	help 	me 	if I 	wanted to get a store location.
Since 	I never really sold anything before, 	I 	had 	some 	apprehension. The 	folks 	at 	US 	Gold 	told
me about 	all 	the sales tools, 	and 	they were right. 	All 	I needed was a 	table. 	As 	a matter 	of fact, 	I
didn't even need a table. I took the chain to work, 	and 	the first week I took 	46 	orders for
deliverv 	on 	oavday. 	All 	T did 	was wear the gold 	chain 	and 	had some samples 	of other styles 	with
me . I 	;old 	it 	dn 	breaks, at 	lunch, 	after work. 	Teven 	held 	some home parties 	using 	guidelines that
US 	Gold set 	up 	in 	their 	manual. 	.
The word had spread that 	I 	could get people beautiful 	14K 	gold-layered jewelry with 	a lifetime 	,
guarantee. People sought 	me 	out. They 	~anted jewelry for themselves, 	as 	well 	as 	gifts. 	As 	soon
as they 	saw 	the quality 	of what 	T was 	weanng, 	they 	were 	sold.
